description:
A small wargame that covers the Spanish Civil War, 1936-1939.  It comes with an 8 page rulebook, map and counters.  It includes 7 different scenarios to reflect different combination of foreign intervention.

Revised as

     Viva España: A Game of the Spanish Civil War – 1936-1939

The box includes a 90-card deck and instructions for 3 different children games:
- Lammbada: A speed game (2-5 players, Ages 6 and up)
- Maeaehmory: A memory game (2-4 players, Ages 7 and up)
- Volle Wolle (Full Wool): (3-6 players, Ages 5 and up)
A wargame on a battle of the Japanese civil war between those factions who wanted to keep Japan closed vs. the Meiji reformers.
family: History: Boshin War & Meiji Era Rebellions

description:
A game from the Pelikan bookcase series.

The game includes two map boards (beginning and advanced) and also includes a plastic divider.

The divider is similar to those used in navigation except this one is fixed and cannot be adjusted. The player places one of the points of the divider in the start hole. The divider is then rotated until a hole matching the radius of the divider is found. There are multiple hole at this radius. One of the holes is chosen and the other point of the divider is rotated until another hole is found and chosen. This continues alternating points of the divider until the goal is reached.

An interesting solo game that seems more like a puzzle. Re-playability is somewhat limited as there are only two different boards.

The game could be extended to multiple players by keeping track of the number of times that the divider points are placed in new holes. The player with the fewest number wins.
Japanese wargame on the Battle of the Coral Sea. Published by Tsukuda hobbies.

Wargame on the 1942 Malaya Campaign in Japanese.

The theme is the invasion of the Malay peninsula by the Japanese army at the earliest stage of the Pacific War, and the player directs the Japanese army or the Commonwealth army of the United Kingdom, and aims to capture / defense the Malay peninsula. [...]

Will the Japanese army occupy the Malay peninsula as historically? Or will the Commonwealth Army retaliate Japan's offensive with a skilled tactics and overthrow history? Please enjoy the "if" of the history that fluctuates by the player's preference.

Two games : "Malay Blitz" and "Capture of Singapore". Both game have different rules.

Second edition components:

    One rule book (32 pages, including "Singapore battle" rules)
    Two maps ("Malay Blitz": B1 size, "Singapore Battle": B3 size)
    2 counter sheets (15 mm square - 396 counters)
    2 card sheets (29 cards + 3 spare cards)
    6 dices


(translated from website and user's description)

A Pelikan bookcase game from their "Buchkassette" series.

This is the basic bingo game played at many locations. Includes the "Gioco Romano" variant where only motives are called not the numbers.
Sun Zi, Japanese multi players game of Chinese Chunjiu Zhanguo era. Published by Epoch.
family: History: Warring States Period of China

description:
Cubical is a fast-paced die rolling racing game with a twist.

The goal of the game is to be the first to move three dice from one side of the board to the opposite overcoming walls and obstacles along the way.

Edition Spielbox No. 62

boardgame: Seven Seas: The Game of Trade
description:
Players roll dice and move their ships from port to port around the world, buying and selling goods specific to each port. (Ex. Players can buy beef in Buenos Aires.)

The mapboard is a map of the entire world and its oceans, with a square grid overlaying the oceans.  Most major ports are depicted.  Some have goods to be bought there, while others are simply destinations for goods.

Game comes with: gameboard, cargo manifests, play money,
pegs, ships, and dice.

Excellent educational game to learn geography.

Ages 12 and up


     of PLAYERS: 2-4 players or teams


OBJECT OF THE GAME:
Players accumulate points by filling in the missing word or words in quotes or by identifying the famous people the quotes are by or about.

EQUIPMENT:
The game includes a card holder, 64 two-sided game cards, two card screens, seven scoring pegs, and a minute timer.

TYPES of QUOTES:
There are three types of quotes in the game--
1) quotes in which one or more words have been replaced by "[BLANK]";
2) quotes about a person to be identified, which have the nouns or pronouns referring to that person underlined;
3) quotes by a person who must be identified

RULES OF PLAY:
Each game card has three categories of quotes. (EX. History, Author, Movers and Shakers) A player (or team) slides down game screen to reveal one quote in one category. All players can hear and see the quote. The current player can make a guess as to the answer, scoring points if correct, and losing points if incorrect.  The player can also pass.  If the player passes or is incorrect, play passes on clockwise to the next player.

OTHER NOTES:
The gamebox says that this stand-alone game is "a sequel to 'Quote, Unquote', chosen 'Best New Trivia Game' by GAMES magazine, 1994."

Can you see a trumpet? How about a rabbit that's pink? Now mark a path between them in this clever game of links! Take turns calling out objects and marking off the spaces. Every board has the same pictures but found in different places. You don't want to help another player win, so be careful what you name. Be the first to link two sides together and Bingo! - You've won the game!

From the publisher:

Be the first to link two sides of the same color -- and win!

There's never a dull moment with Picture Link, as every player is involved on every move.

Players take turns calling out pictures and marking them bingo-style as they try to make a path from one side of their game board to the other.

Each game board has the same pictures in different locations, so with each call you may be helping another player as well as yourself!

Package description:
Dolly & Daniel, the two frisky little whales, usually play with the Eskimos on the icebergs of the Arctic Ocean. However, once every year, they travel to the Antarctic to visit their friends, the penguins. As they move along, they meet some friendly "spouting" whales, who help them. They also encounter some polar bears, who delay them, and 'the' angry angler who sends them back. 
The girl players are "Dolly" whales and the boys are "Danny" whales. 
The object of the game is to be the first whale to reach the Antarctic.

boardgame: Legion: Wargame Rules for Ancient Battles on the Grand Tactical Scale
description:
Wargame Rules for Ancient Battles on the Grand Tactical Scale

Players write down their moves and then move simultaneously.
family: History: Greek & Persian conflicts of the 4th century BC
History: Wars in the Ancient Near East
History: Wars of Alexander the Great
History: Wars of the Diadochi and Syrian Wars

description:
Louise and the Wild Goats (Luise und die wilden Ziegen) is a tactical die-rolling game.

The goal of the game is to drive your goats from the pasture into the barn making effective use of guard dog Louise. The way and number of spaces Louise moves on the board depends on the die roll. If you roll a 2, for example, Louise may jump over one or two goats Halma style. If Louise ends up on a space occupied by a goat, the goat moves one space straight on. The 12 barn spaces are worth victory points from 1 to 3. The game ends when the barn is fully filled with goats. The player with the most victory points wins the game.

Edition Spielbox No. 63

Star Blazers is a very simple hex-and-counter game, based on the famous Japanese anime series Space Battleship Yamato (Uchuu Senkan Yamato). The human player/s have to make a journey from Earth to the planet Iscandar and back within a limit of 26 turns (one year simulated), to save the home planet from deadly radioactivity. The Gamilon player/s have to delay the Yamato and his escort ships, coming in their way, making their path more hazardous with the use of mines and shooting missiles.

The game movement, shooting and counter-attacks are managed by a spinner. Special damage and events are activated by the 8 cards.

Game components:
1 game board
1 plastic Yamato stand-up
5 plastic spaceships
5 round plastic parts for spaceships
1 plastic turn counter stand-up
8 special events/damages cards
30 hex counters missiles
30 hex counters mines
Stickers for the Yamato stand-up and for the turn counter
1 spinner

Move it! is a simple game simulating the transport of containers at the Europe Combined Terminals. You have to move little wooden containers from a boat to one of a maximum of six stacks (rows) and then onto a truck to be transported over land. Players can block each other by stacking containers on top of each other. Decreasing the amount of stacks in play makes the game harder.

The game was commissioned by ECT (European Container Terminal) in Rotterdam, The Netherlands, on the occasion of the completion of the Delta/Sea-Land Terminal.

From a translation of the rules:

Kreuzweise was designed to play at home or when away. It is compact and the optimized material chosen for the high quality playing cards is thin, flexible and easy to clean. Where ever you play Kreuzweise, enjoy!

Basically, it's a tile-laying puzzle game where points are scored based upon the number of matching edges.

Players take the roles of antique dealers bidding for items at auction.  At the start of the game each player secretly selects eight items, represented by nicely illustrated cards, of the sixteen different antiques available (the deck includes three of each type) recording these on a score pad.  Each item is likely to have a different value, representing the resale value for each player.  The object therefore is to purchase as many of the selected items as possible for less than the resale price.

Players take it in turns to auction the cards.  The progress of each auction is unusual in being determined by die rolls. The auctioneer rolls a die to "establish the reserve". The next player in rotation can either roll again, the value shown being added to the reserve price or drop out.  This continues until only one player remains in the bidding. The cost of the item is then the sum of all the die rolls!

Items other than the eight may be purchased but  at the game end they will represent a loss there is no resale value) and at any point a player may offer an item or items for sale at a price to be negotiated.
The game continues until one player has accumulated his or her
portfolio of eight items, the game ends immediately and all players calculate their profits.

The largest profit wins.

'The New Property Development Board Game': in contrast to Monopoly presumably. Play comprises two stages.  In stage one players travel around a a perimeter track, 'the inner ring road' on the roll of two dice, collect income on one corner and pay taxes on another.  Each space represents a parking space.  These give either an option to purchase either property  or pick up a card and follow the instructions on it. The object is to collect a complete site made up of ten back-land plots together with a drainage easement.  When a player has achieved this, movement moves to the outer (ring road?) track that is meant to represent the process of obtaining Planning Approval for the development. This is achieved by firstly collecting cards giving recommendations for approval from three separate local authority departments and then being the first to land on the town hall space: yes, on the roll of the dice and, yes, doubles mean another go!

Front line by Bandai
This is a man to man level tactical combat in WW2.
Some gamers ranks this game high, especially when the details of weapon is concerned.
Another feature is there are self adhesive terrain stickers are included.
They stick to the mapboard (hard board) and are easily pealed off so they  are re-usable.
This game has companion game called "Shin-ei-tai", which means "the Guards" or "SS".

This game is intended to be an "introductory" wargame for newcomers.

description:
Spots is a card game in which two or more players will make change from a pool of cards.  Scoring is handicapped based on the difference between the total of a player's starting cards and the lowest total of the opponent's starting cards.

Players put one card from their hand into the pool, taking cards with a total value equal to or greater into their score pile.  The player with the lowest score after all cards have been played wins.

Spots was designed to be a card-based version of Pennywise.  It was first published in the Origins 2000 preregistration book.

This is a Japanese game on "Operation Barbarossa."

This game won the 1st prize in the design competition presented by Sho-Kikaku.

A remake Blitzkrieg to Moscow 2 was published in 2016.

description:
The Cuban Revolution depicts the critical area and time in the Guerrilla war fought between the colonial government of Fulgencio Batista and the Guerrillas led by charismatic individuals like Fidel Castro and Ernesto “Che” Guevara. Beginning in February, 1958 Castro began the first Guerrilla offensive by coming out of the Sierra Maesta Mountains and attacking the Bastiano troops in the plains. The Bastiano army counterattacked driving the Guerrillas back into the mountains beginning in June 1958. When the Bastiano army attempted to destroy the guerrillas, the Bastiano army was all but destroyed itself. With all the newly acquired weapons, the guerrillas launched a final offensive that ended in the only major battle of the war at the town of Santa Clara. The defeat of the Bastiano western forces caused the rest of the army to begin deserting. When Batista fled in January 1959, the guerrillas triumphantly declared the island was finally free. The improbable victory of the Guerrillas resulted in the only communist country in the Western Hemisphere.

The Cuban Revolution uses area movement and a simple and easy-to-learn combat system. Learning the game takes minutes and about 1-2 hours of playing time at most.

description:
In the early morning hours of March 6, 1836, after a thirteen-day siege, the besieging Mexican army surrounding San Antonio, Texas, launched a surprise attack upon the presidio of the Alamo, which was defended by U.S., Texan and Tejano volunteer militia units. The cheering Mexican soldiers, along with the accompanying bugle call of the “Deguello”, soon betrayed the surprise attack. Within an hour and a half, the entire garrison of 182 to 225 volunteers had been “put to the sword,” and General Santa Anna had won his victory at the cost of some 311 of his brave officers and soldiers. Even more, he created an American myth that began with the “Deguello at Dawn”

Dennis Bishop uses area movement, facing, ranged fire and melee combat to recreate this famous event in American History. Leaders play a big role in the game and rules for Col. James Bowie to determine if he's healthy and able to take part in the battle.

A unique twist on casualties is also used as the game has a large map (17" x 22") and uses over 400 double sided unit counters (some that are 1½" x ½") as well as about 8-10 pages of rules and various game player aids.

Deguello at Dawn was a nominee in the Charles S. Roberts awards for Best DTP-Produced Boardgame (Charles S. Roberts Awards) for 2005.

description: The object of the game is to catch marbles through holes in a vertical maze as the marbles fall from top to bottom.

Space Race is a simple card game designed for two players, though more can play with additional card sets (as there is no player interaction whatsoever).

The object of the game is to complete a round trip through the Solar System by drawing ten planet cards in their proper order. (The order will be unfamiliar, though, as it goes Earth -> Saturn -> Jupiter -> Mars -> Uranus -> Neptune -> Venus -> Mercury -> Earth). Each card features well executed art done in a style similar to golden age science fiction pulp magazines.

The game consists of two identical decks with cards numbered 1-10. These cards represent round trips the the Solar System, starting with "Take Off" (Card 1) and ending with "Landing On Earth" (Card 10). Players take a deck and shuffle it, then place it before them. The first player draws a card. If it is "Take Off" (Card 1), they place it in front of them and draw the next card. If the next card is a planet but not Card 2, they discard it in a discard pile next to their original pile and draw again, until they draw a card that says "Next player goes". The next player takes a turn, and draws until they get a "Next player goes" card. Chance cards such as "lost on the moon," "Sputnik," and "Danger--Comets" can affect a player's turn.

Players may draw from either pile, so there is a memory element, as you must remember which pile your next planetary target was in.

All cards must be discarded in the pile next to the pile from which they were drawn.

The goal is to collect all 10 cards, in sequence.

Rules list a variant for three or more players through using multiple copies of the game.

Whenever a Medabot is destroyed, players pay a cost in Hit Points. The player who causes his opponent to lose all his HP is the winner.

This card game is intended to be played while watching a televised basketball contest. Each player is dealt a hand of seven cards, each with a basketball "event" such as slam dunk, blocked shot, free throw, and rebound.  When a player has a card that matches the event that just occurred on the televised game, they must be the first to play it, thereby scoring the points on the card for that event (the rarer the event the more points). When a player plays all seven of their cards, he gets a seven point bonus, everyone tallies their scores and a new hand is dealt. Play continues until the end of the game and the player with the most points from completed rounds is the winner.

If you land on a puddle space, you lose a turn. The first player to reach the balloon space at the end of the track wins.

Ages 10 - 16

The games is played in rounds, where the dealer plays a number of cards face-up, and one face-down. Players decide whether the round will be lottery-style (person with the most matches to the dealer's hand wins), or poker-style (bidding on your hand plus cards from the dealer's hand).

This is a "Cootie" type game in which players try to assemble one of the Mickey Mouse Club characters. On your turn you spin a spinner to see what body parts you may add. This children's pizza game, with a Mickey Mouse theme, is actually 4 games-in-one. In the first game, you pop the popper die and find the pizza that matches the shape popped. You "deliver" the pizza to the character whose hands are shown on the card. The player who delivers the last pizza wins. Game #2 is a memory game. You pop the popper and turn over a pizza card. If it matches the shape and color of the pizza you popped, you take the character card that ordered that pizza. The player with the most character cards at the end wins. Game #3 is a typical memory game where players try to get the most matching pairs. Game #4 is an advanced version of Game #1.

One year after releasing the highly-regarded game and art object Die HolzerSiebeN by Friedensreich Hundertwasser, Kosmos is releasing another art game, this time with James Rizzi - albeit in a much lower price category.

The game idea, invented by Reiner Knizia, is very simple but tense. The 28 large Rizzi art cards have values between 0 and 6; each of the two players receives twelve. On a player's turn, they must discard a card from their hand. To discard, a player must either create a new discard pile or place the card on an existing discard pile - but in that case, the value of the card must be exactly one higher than the card onto which it is to be placed. If there are already three discard piles on the table, then a player has no choice but to discard to one of the piles. If he can't, then he has lost the round and receives as many glass beads as cards left in his hand. The first player to get six glass beads is the loser.

Was initially supposed to be published by KOSMOS - but has only been published as PnP rules.

Paul and Paula are the fastest pizza deliverers around. Their good reputation is influenced by the fact that pizzas occasionally slip out of their hands. Clumsiness? Nope! Paul and Paula can simply only deliver pizzas that they are magnetically attracted to. Pizzas of the wrong pole rebuff Paul and Paula. The two of them need a little help. Someone will surely remember which pizzas the two of them should be delivering. The game includes a round wooden game board that looks like a pizza with an arrow spinner in the middle, nine magnetic pizza pieces and two magnetic figures. The two figures, Paul and Paula, have different poles (positive and negative) to ensure that they are never attracted to the same pizza. The spinner determines which figure a player, on their turn, may hold over a pizza piece. If they choose correctly, the pizza, with a click, fastens itself to the figure. The winner is the player who owns three pizza pieces first.

boardgame: Mighty Morphin Power Rangers Audio Game
description: This is actually 2 games that use an audio cassette to direct the game play. Game #2 (Side B on the tape) is a more complex version of Game #1 (Side A on the tape). To start each player takes a board that depicts all 5 Teen Heroes. The tape will tell you when to draw cards and when to battle. The object is to cover your Heroes with their Power Ranger counterpart cards. A Rita Repulsa card may be played on an opponent to make him or her lose a turn. A Putty card is immediately discarded (thus wasting a draw). A Space Alien card may be placed on an opponents Power Ranger card forcing that player to play another Ranger card on that space. A Megazord card defeats a Space Alien and/or prevents further attacks on that space. The first player to morph all the Teen Heroes on his or her game board into the corresponding Power Rangers wins.

From the publisher:

Four angry families quarrel in the labyrinth-like garden about the fruit. The fathers send their sons to collect the fruit while they try to steal it from the other's sons. The maze changes with every turn and if you don't pay attention, you'll get nothing home at the end.

From the publisher's website:

You try to build bridges on your construction-site. This results in real chaos and soon the first parts will be falling to the ground. Try to keep your eye on your strategy to get the highest score. Limited edition of 150 pieces, numbered and signed by the author.

Each player gets four little plots on the back of the game-box. Depending on the number of empty plots, a player gets material at the beginning of his turn. Now he can use this material to build connections between his plots. There are different building-styles which are more or less difficult and therefore result in more or less points. A player can also use the already built parts of the other player, which is easier and therefore results in less points. If you manage to build very complicated connections, you'll get more points. After a while, the board will be full of chaotically arranged bridges. If something falls, you'll get penalty, so be careful! Once all plots are connected, the game is over and if you didn't plan carefully you again get a penalty.

boardgame: Mighty Morphin Power Rangers: War of the Zords
description: This game, featuring a Power Rangers theme, is similar to the card game "War" with a few added twists. The deck is dealt out face down evenly among the players. Cards depict Power Rangers, bad guys, or puzzle pieces. Each player simultaneously turns over their top card and the player with the highest value card wins them all. If there's a tie, the tying players continue turning over cards one at a time until someone turns over a higher value card. The player that wins the trick takes all point cards and places them in a separate pile (to be counted up later). Then the winner collects all puzzle cards and puts them in the center of the table either starting or adding to a puzzle. There are 2 six-piece puzzles: one depicting a Megazord, and one depicting Rita Repulsa. The game ends when either puzzle is completed. If the Megazord puzzle is completed, the point values on Power Ranger cards are added up. If the Rita Repulsa puzzle is completed, the point values on bad guy cards are added up. The player with the most points is the winner.

The game is a round trip through Switzerland, departing from and returning to Zürich.  Distance is determined by die roll and there are no branches in the route, the only option being to choose to travel clockwise or counter-clockwise.  Each player has six place cards, and if he or she stops at a place owned by another must pay that person one chip or miss a turn.  The winner is the player with the highest total of chips plus bonus points gained by early completion of the journey.

Publication date is not specified but is probably early 1960s.  The box cites both Franz Carl Weber/Jouets Weber (Swiss toy company and chain of shops) and Otto Maier Verlag (later Ravensburger: the box is printed "Ravensburger Spiele Nr. 23").  Otto Maier published a different game of the same name in 1891.

The Stars Our Decimation is the name for a series of interrelated science fiction wargames built around the actions mechanic. TSOD games all feature hand-built rules, hex grid regulated movement, a unique actions system, and versatile unit design rules.

Voidstriker is one of the games in the series.  It is a fleet-oriented game of starship combat. Ships and fighters battle in deep space and in the gravity wells of important worlds. The rules feature unique, playable vector movement with gravity effects around planets, versatile ship design rules, quick and detailed combat resolution, and easy adaptation to use of miniatures or starship models.

Ground Zero is another game in the series.  It is a tactical ground combat game, best suited to running company-level combats. Rules are based strongly on Voidstriker, so if you know one, you'll have a pretty good handle on the other.

Board (6x4)
Players should agree whether they play with rows of size 6 and columns of size 4, or with rows of size 4 and columns of size 6.

Rules
Players each have the following pieces: one king, one queen, and two pawns. They may setup their pieces as they want on the first rank of their side of the board.

It's probably better if players alternately setup one of their pieces, with the kings deployed last. Using a screen in the middle of the board behind which the pieces can be set up secretly may also be an idea, but poses both practical difficulties.

King and Queen move as in orthodox chess.

Pawns have the usual moves of chess, but may also move diagonally.

Quotes from the box:

"A game that brings you the space battles of Star Wars with three levels of play..."

Issue one of the ATS Briefing magazine, containing a standalone ATS basic game.  "1940 Airborne Assault at the Albert Canal", featuring Germans vs Belgians, 2 counter sheets, six scenarios, mapsheet, v1.5 of the ATS Basic rules.  Also contains two scenarios for Scottish Corridor.

boardgame: Stock Rush! A Week on Wall Street
description:
BRIEF SUMMARY:

Stock Rush is a Strategy Board Game that uses an Interactive Device called the "Market Display".  PLAYERS WATCH THEIR STOCKS FALL, RISE, CRASH and SKYROCKET . . . RIGHT BEFORE THEIR EYES! For added excitement, players also Mine for Gold and participate in a Gold Rush!  The "Game Play" in Stock Rush is so unique and diverse that it spans many game categories including: Strategy, Family, Party, Simulation, Educational, Action and Adventure!
  
For 2 to 10 Players, Ages 8 and up.

DETAILED SUMMARY:

Game play begins on The Opening Bell space and a peg is used to keep track of the trading day.  Players can play a regular game and spend a full business week on Wall Street [Mon-Fri] or they can play a shorter game by starting on Wednesday.  The game usually ends when the trading day peg reaches “Game Over” which is the slot after Friday.  Other ways the game can end are: “Market Collapse” in which all companies go bankrupt; One player left in the game after all the other players go bankrupt and All players are trapped in a Cave In.  At the end of the game, all players liquidate [sell] their holdings to the bank and the player with the most cash wins the game.

STOCKS . . .

Players buy and sell [Trade] stocks at the current market price shown by the "Market Display" [4 Stocks comprise the market in the game, A,B,C and D]. The stock prices are affected by news and rumors that randomly occur throughout the course of the game. There are two types of news and rumors that occur: Market News and Rumors affect the entire market; and Company News and Rumors which only affect a given individual company. Although rumors occur at random; how long they remain alive can be strategically predicted. The longer a rumor remains alive the higher the chance it will be confirmed or dispelled on the next turn. Consequently, players must trade accordingly; they must decide on 4 Criteria: [1] Buy or Sell?; [2]Which Stock to Trade?; [3] How many Shares to Trade?; and most importantly [4] When to Trade?.

COMMODITIES [MAINLY GOLD] . . .

The Stock Rush name was inspired by the California Gold Rush of 1849. As a result, a significant portion of the game is dedicated to that historical event. Players randomly mine for Gold and participate in many Gold Rushes throughout the game. During a “Gold Rush” players may become trapped in a “Cave In”.  Trapped players may escape on their own or be rescued by other players.  
Once players accumulate Gold they may strategically sell any amount they wish on the "Gold Exchange Market". They must sell according to 3 Criteria:
[1] How much Gold to Sell? [2] At What Price to Sell? [3] When to Sell?  The other commodities in the game are Groceries, Toys, Clothes and Gear…the STUFF we use in our everyday life.

When Mighty Mouse reaches your space, he releases you and any other player caught in a hazard. The first player to reach the Finish space by exact count wins. If you cannot make a play, you draw a card instead. The other players also add letters to the word (if possible) on their turns. When the word "Millionaire" is spelled out, the player having the fewest cards left in his or her hand scores points equal to all the cards left in the opponent's hands. The first player to score 75 points is the winner. Rules for playing "Card Anagrams" are also included.

Milton is an electronic game where players try to complete phrases. It plays 3 different games but all of them have the same basic mechanics. On your turn, you press a red phrase button and listen as Milton speaks the start of a phrase like "Blink your". You then press one of the yellow answer buttons and listen to the response (in this case you're trying to find "eyes"). If you are correct, you take another turn. If you are incorrect, your turn ends and Milton ridicules you. When all the phrases have been matched with their endings, the player who made the most matches wins.

[FROM THE RULES]

The game of modern masters: Impressionism to Surrealism.

ARTDECK is a game that offers art lovers and game players aesthetic pleasure and an exciting way to learn about modern art.  Play the game as directed, play other games of your own, or just enjoy the images on the cards.  ARTDECK will enrich your leisure hours.

A 3-D strategic games with rhombic dodecahedrons to build a pyramid.
Each piece piece has 6 white sides and six black sides.
The contents are 30 plastic pieces, 2 playing grounds (triangular and square), and instructions.
The main game (Cuckoo) is to place your piece at the top of the pyramid.  You can prevent your opponent from reaching that goal through clever piece placement.

Variants include Zebra and Ant rules.

This is a set of rules for Minatures in the Napoleonic era. Originally published in 1971 as Napoleonique, it was revised and reissued in 1992 as Napoleonique Encore.

Here is the description from the back of the Encore edition: 
"Napoleonique /Encore covers the wars of the French Revolution through the many campaigns of Napoleon and to the final battle of Waterloo in 1815.  You select the battalion or regimental game from scales set up to accommodate your current mounting scheme.  The focus on simplicity will have you finishing a turn in twenty minutes with armies of a thousand or more.
The original rules called Napoleonique, set battalion level games at a scale of thirty men per casting, a ten minute time scale and twenty yards per inch.  Napoleonique/Encore offers 5 game scales with casting representing 30 men in some scales up to 90 men in the regimental version.  These rule shave a history of being the beginners friend and the veteran's "first car" in wargamming.
Napoleonique/Encore is a product of the team of Jim Getz and Glenn Davis.  Over 20,000 copies of the original Napoleonique were sold since its release in the early 70's.  Some of you may buy Napoleonique/Encore for nostalgic reasons while others will find the simplicity to be a pleasant change.  Either way we know you'll enjoy these rules."

description:
"Rules book for large-scale Ancient wargaming  with miniatures. Includes 11x17 cardstock play-aid with charts. Zip locked."- from the 24-page book

"These rules are designed to give players the feel of Ancient warfare on a large scale. They include the most commonly used units and their capabilities to allow you to make up the Ancient army of your choice and get to playing quickly." - from the introduction

This game's subtitle, 'A playful introduction to the people and cultures of Africa' is perhaps overstating things. This game consists of rolling and moving around the board collecting things that are supposed to tell us about Africa (coconuts, tree, necklace, fish, mask, drum, hut...). Each player has a board with pictures of 9 of the 16 objects to be collected and rolls the die to move around the board and fill up her or his board. The only further 'complication' is that there are some arrow squares on the board, some pointing clockwise, some anticlockwise. It would appear that landing on these squares can change the direction the player is going in. Landing on any other square gets one of the objects, if its one the player needs.

This is intended as a child's first game. My copy is missing the rulebook, but it isn't hard to surmise the basic rules.

Same as Catch Phrase but words or phrases that appear on the readout are familiar to adults and well as children. Anyone who can read can play.  Players sit in a circle and divide teams into every other person. One person is selected as the starting player or "Clue Giver." After reading the "target word" on the game's display, the clue giver yells out associated words, hints, sounds until his/her team guesses the target word. As soon as this happens the player presses the Next button and passes the game device to next player who must now give clues to his/her team.  All the while the timer is ticking down. Beep...Beep...Beep..Beep..Beep.Beep.BeepBeepBeep! 
If your team is not in possession of the game device when the buzzer goes off and you score! First team to 7 wins!!
family: Versions & Editions: Junior Versions of Grown-Up Games

description: This game recreates The Miss America Pageant with players competing in the Swimsuit, Talent, Personality and Evening Gown competitions. The game uses a spinner shaped to look like a television camera. On your turn, you spin the spinner and advance the number of spaces indicated. If you land on a competition space, you draw a card that tells you what score you received for that event. When you have cards from all the competitions, you place your contestant piece on one of the 3 Semi-Final spaces and start a new contestant. It is possible for a player to have 0, 1, 2 or 3 contestants in the Semi-Final judging. When all 3 Semi-Finalist spaces are filled, the player with the highest point score moves her contestant to a Finalist space. A new round is then played and so on until all 5 Finalist spaces are filled around the base of the camera. The spinner is then spun and the contestant in front of the camera pointer is named 4th runner up and removed. This process is repeated eliminating 1 contestant at a time. The remaining contestant is named Miss America, and the player who controls her wins the game.
August the Butterfly is a symmetry learning game for young children Ages 4 and up

This is an electronic reaction game with a "Monsters Inc." theme. Players choose a colored Scream Canister and sit directly behind it. On top of the game is a door with Mike and Sulley standing next to it. As play begins, the top will whirl and as the door opens, a scream, laugh, or voice will be heard. You need to hit the Scream Canister in front of you to "capture" a scream (for 1 point) or a laugh (for 2 points). The player who presses the canister first gets the point(s). If Mike's voice is heard, a player pressing a canister loses a point. The first player to score 10 points is the winner.

This game is presented by the Economist and sponsored by major companies that are represented in the game, like AT&T, UPS and Shangri-La Hotels. It is meant to give players a flavor of how a competitive market works, while familiarizing them with economic terms and concepts.

Each player represents an "Enterpriser" who begins a small business and attempts to be the first to build a financial empire. Players have to decide how and when to invest, how much to borrow, how much inventory to maintain and when to take advantage of special opportunities that may come along through contacts with other companies; all this amidst changing economic times and unpredictable events (manifested through event cards).

This is a Japanese wargame on the Battle of Britain 1940. This is published by Epoch.

Do not confuse with Epoch Wargame Electronics #4: The Battle of Britain.

description:
The first player to move along the pathway through biblical scenes from Creation to Christ wins the game.  The object of the game is to bring families together to share and learn their biblical faith in a friendly atmosphere of fun and competitive play.

Players chose in advance which color group or groups of Question and Answer cards they want to play.  Players also decide which level of difficulty each one will play.  A player remains at that level of difficulty (Elementary, Intermediate or Advanced) throughout the game.

This game, based on the Mickey Mouse Club, uses a 3-D cardboard Mouseketeer Club House as a game board. Each player uses a peg to climb up one side of the house. On your turn, you spin the spinner and "climb" the number of spaces indicated. When you reach the Mickey Mouse space at the top, you spin again. If you spin one of the numbers shown under Mickey, you take a scoring disc off the roof of the clubhouse and return your peg to the starting position where you'll start your next turn. After the last disc has been removed from the roof the game ends. The player whose discs add up to the highest score wins.

This game features an electronic talking Mr. Potato Head that gives the players clues. Fifteen cards showing Mr. Potato Head in various outfits are placed face-up on the table. Each card has a unique combination of 3 special items that no other card contains. On your turn, you press a Special Part button (such as mustache or glasses) and Mr. Potato Head will tell you if the picture he's thinking of contains that item. If it doesn't, you turn all the pictures that have that item face down. If it does, you turn all the pictures that don't have that item face down. If you select a correct item, you may also guess at the picture by pressing 3 Special Part buttons for the unique parts that are in the one you think is correct. If you guess right, you win.
This is a Japanese tactical board game set in the anime world of Captain Harlock and Galaxy Express 999.

This game features Mr. Ribbit, an electronic plastic frog who sits on a large central lily pad. Nine smaller lily pads are placed in a circle around it. The small pads have a picture of either a bug or a gator on their bottoms. On your turn, you pick up Mr. Ribbit and place him on any small lily pad. If he says "Sproing!", hop him to the next lily pad and so on around the circle. When he's through "sproinging", he will say "Rrribbit! Who's there?" You must then guess if the lily pad he's on has a bug or a gator underneath. If you guess correctly, you keep the lily pad. If Mr, Ribbit says "Splash!", move him to any lily pad of your choice. The first player to collect 3 lily pads is the winner. A second game may be played where players collect bug tokens instead of lily pads.
Players roll dice to determine two letters. The player who first finds a word with one of the letters as first letter and the other letter as last letter gets points.

Timon - the bewitched steering wheel - is a simple dice game in which players try to capture a counter from each opponent to win the game. This is done by moving your opponents' pieces across the spokes of a nautical wheel to a capture spot on your "own" spoke. Movement is determined by rolling 2 dice, one to determine which color can be moved, the other for range. Rolling your own color or the neutral white allows you can move any piece of your opponents. The rules include a variation that permits blocking by stacking pieces on top of each other, hiding the pieces underneath.

A pleasant and simple dice game entry in the Edition Perlhuhn range, played on an interesting and atmospheric board with luck being a major factor.

1 year later described in Reinhold Wittig's SPIELECOLLECTION No. 1 Spiele zur Schatzinsel (1988)
as "Das verflixte Steuerrad".

In addition to the rules, it includes a book, 24 cards, and a cardboard clock which are used in the various games. boardgame: The Littles Pop-up and Play Game
description:
The Littles Pop-up and Play Game is a Milton Bradley board game that is based on the early 80s television series, "The Littles", which is based on a series of children's books by John Peterson.  The Littles are little 4 inch tall humanoid creatures that live in the walls of human's homes.

The board was rather interesting because it had pop up sections that made up The Littles' living area.  The object of the game is to collect some inventions and get them to Dinky Littles' room.

This game, based on the Mr. T cartoon show, plays like the card game "War" with a few extra twists. The cards depict various characters from the show and a small board is used to organize them as they're played. The entire deck is dealt out evenly among the players into face down piles. Players then draw the top 3 cards to form their hands. In a preliminary round, players take the top card from their piles and play them onto their board space. If no cards match but one is a Mr. T card, the player who played Mr. T wins all the cards. If two or three cards match, a showdown takes place in which players play cards from their hands trying to be the only player to match one of the non-matching cards played by another player. The winner gets all the cards played. If you lose all your cards you're out. When only 2 players remain, the one with the greatest number of cards wins. Rules for playing 2-player are included.

This game is no longer in production.
If replacement pieces are needed, please contact SimplyFun. We'll do our best to replace game pieces as long as they are available in our warehouse.

Takes the basic idea of Bunco and adds a house building element.  Players roll special dice, then build parts of their house if the correct symbols were drawn.  Roll dice that did not allow you to build and you must pass the dice to the next player or set of players.  Roll 3 of a kind and you must start over.

From original website -

Bringing Down the House™ 
The Groundbreaking Dice Rolling Game

Choose your partner, grab the dice and get ready to roll! Bringing Down the House will have everyone shaking in their seats.

Compete against the other players to roll the right combination of dice, at just the right time, to build your house. 
Roll 3 of a kind and the Foundation will shake and you must start over. Finish first and ring the bell to win!

From the box:
"60 cards with colored shapes on a see through background.  Different parts of each card are colored in such a way that when the right cards are held together they make a solid image that cannot be seen through.  Sometimes two cards are needed, sometimes 3, 4 or 5.  The game is especially suited to help develop the faculties of concentration, observation and combination.  Adults will find fascinating entertainment as the game provides ample scope for tactics."

For 3 to 6 players, also possible for 1 player.

boardgame: Rook Plus: The Wild Bird Game
description:
This game might be described as a cross between Rook and BINGO. Each player has a Rook Plus game card, which is divided into 16 spaces (4x4 grid), each with a color and number that corresponds with a card in the Rook deck. As tricks are taken, players mark captured cards on their game card. Players win by filling four spaces in a row (horizontally, vertically, or diagonally.

Contents

     57 Rook Cards (3additional cards about Parker Brothers but not part of the game)
     4 Wild Bird Score Cards
     50 scoring chips
     English instructions

"Wargaming Rules for Heroic Battles and Mystical Conquests."

A comprehensive (56 pages) set of miniatures rules for conducting warfare in a fantasy setting. Both combat and magic are used to resolve battles. The rules are designed for standard 25mm miniatures, but guidance is also given for playing with 15mm miniatures.

The rules "comprehensiveness" is readily apparent: almost every aspect of any epic fantasy battle in any story ever published in any century is covered. Really. Behold a random sampling from the rules' index:


     Ceremonial Magic
     Bombards
     Metamorphoids
     Sacrificial Victims 
     Chariots
     Oblique movement of self-disciplined units
     Rules for innate chi projection
     Killing rampaging large mounts
     Infernal Engine Disadvantages   
     Unconscious and mad magic users
     Aerial unit fatigue
     "Goetic Magic Hour Correspondence Charts"
     Effects of routing units on dray animals
     Klipothic and Beneficient Magic


The rules' color text claims that the magic system "is based on the actual grimoires (magical texts) that guided medieval European thaumaturges (workers of miracles)." Cardstock sheets are designed to be cut into individual spell cards marked with "evocation circles, mystical symbols, demons and angels that have been familiar to magic users for centuries." It is hard to judge the historical accuracy of such statements, but the book is packed with so many spell charts, Astral Force cards, magic critical fail results, and the affects of "High Magic Color" rules that Merlin would probably sell his left one for just one peek at the last 30 pages of the book.

description: The game is over when all spaces of the gameboard are occupied. The player with most influence gets points. The player with the most points wins.

boardgame: The Game of Big Rig 18 Wheeler
description:
This is a relatively sophisticated roll-and-move trucking game. Players each choose one of 12 major cities as their home terminal and draw a dispatch card with two possible destinations. They choose one, get paid and pay expenses, then set off. When they reach their chosen destination, they draw a new dispatch card from that terminal. If their destination is not a terminal, then they first must deadhead to the nearest terminal before drawing a card. There are 11 dispatch cards for each of the terminal cities.

As the game progresses, players can buy more trucks, up to four of them, at $60,000 each. They can borrow up to $50,000 but must make minimum payments on every die roll until it is paid back with interest. The winner is the player to that makes the most money (including truck values) by the end of 3 hours or whatever other time limit may be agreed at the outset.

The roads contain a variety of hazards, from minor annoyances like D.o.T. inspections and flat tires ($100) to a blown engine ($6,500) or a wreck and hospital stay ($8,000). Dispatch cards may be for regular loads (which use a 1 ,2 ,4, 8, 9, 10 green die), or wide loads that move on a slower 1, 3, 4, 5, 5, 7 red die. Players may buy insurance against cargo damage (or not) and occasionally get asked to take an illegal Hot Load, which pays well if delivered on time but nothing if late or stopped at a scale or inspection point.

Players passing through Atlantic City, Reno or Las Vegas have the option of gambling, drawing from a deck of Chance cards.
This is a Japanese naval wargame on the Battle of Santa Cruz during World War 2. Uses the same system as Opertion MI.

Stalk-1 is the first incorporation of 1/285th Scale Miniature vehicles into a boxed wargame.  The game is designed to demonstrate the aesthetics of miniatures for tactical wargaming.

The game comes with 4 8x10 modular hex based mapboards and 10 1/285th scale German and Russian metal tank miniatures (from CinC).  A unique movement system has one player secretly plot his/her moves, then the other player makes their moves directly on the mapboard, after which the plotted moves are executed and combat can take place by either player at any time during the execution of the plotted moves.  Turret direction (and speed determined by gun size) are factors in the combat system.

description:
Olympia, the official game of the 1972 Olympics in München, Germany, is a dice rolling racing game. The board depicts the emblem of the München Olympic Games. First person to move two tokens from the periphery to the middle of the board is the winner.

Olympia is part of the Ravensburger Casino Series, a ‘bookshelf’ line of games published in the Seventies.

boardgame: New Kids On The Block Game
description: This simple track game is themed after the boy band of the same name. On your turn you roll the die and move the number of spaces indicated. If you roll "New Kids On The Block", you draw a card and play a card from your hand (these send you to a specific space). If you land on a space with the name of a New Kid, you take a framed picture of that Kid from the supply. When you have 3 or more pictures you may try to win by landing on the Finish space. You then roll the die and add the number of pictures you've collected to the number rolled. If the total is 10 or more, you win.

This is a children's puzzle building game with a Sesame Street theme. Each piece in a puzzle has a number on it. To begin, you must spin a "1" or "Wild" on the spinner. You then take the #1 piece of a puzzle and place it in front of you. After that, you may collect pieces in any order, assembling them as you collect them. Each area on the spinner has two numbers. You may collect either numbered piece indicated (but not both). Spinning the Wild space let's you collect any piece. The first player to complete his or her puzzle wins. An advanced game requires players to build 2 puzzles.

18IB is 18xx set on the Iberian peninsula. Two sets of rules are available: a basic version which is simpler than 1830: Railways & Robber Barons and an expanded version which is more historical and includes different gauges.

boardgame: Bulls, Bears N' Bellies: Commodity Futures Trading Game
description:
This is a bookshelf-style game of commodity futures trading.

The authors describe it thusly: "The game is devised to be fun, educational, and fast paced. It is developed in such a way that the uninitiated trader who knows nothing about the factors that affect prices of commodity futures contracts can learn, in a very basic way, about these factors by studying the information and the accompanying price changes on the cards."

"The exciting new board game you play while watching or listening to a live baseball game on TV or radio.  Just watch or listen and predict what will happen after each pitch and batter.  Will the next pitch be hit foul?  Will this batter strike out or hit a home run?  Did you make the correct prediction?  Then move the spaces awarded.  Make the wrong call?  You're out!  Yell at the umpire more that the manager! Players are on the edge of their seats from pitch to pitch!  
It's a whole new way to enjoy baseball!
First player around the board wins.  A Winner is usually determined after three innings, but could be over after just one batter, depending on the predictions.
Predict-a-Ball scores big with baseball fans of all ages.  Don't just watch the game.  Get into the Game!" -from the back of the box
This is an expansion kit for Mobile Suit Gundam ZZ: Side One, and can not play standalone. This kit add space combat rule and 40 new weapons.

In There Oughta Be A Law, each player is a Senator in a committee, trying to make the most money by voting for bills various special interest groups support.

There are two types of cards: bills and events. Bills can be played as amendments to an existing bill. Events modify interest groups positions on bills or alter a players options for play. Each bill lists the interest groups' positions on bills. Players can add hidden amendments to prevent other players from gaining interest group support.

The player with the most money received from interest groups at the end of the game wins.

Published by Sacred Cow Games, 2000.

The object of the game is to earn more points than any other players by building an ark, loading the ark with 8 pairs of animals, plus Noah and Mrs. Noah and by doing good deeds.

Players start each turn with six cards and must play as many cards as possible. The player has a "corral" where he can hold up to four singles or pairs (including Mr. and Mrs. Noah). When all three parts of the ark are complete, up to eight pairs may be moved on the ark, plus Mr. and Mrs. Noah. When one player has a full ark, the game ends.

Players can play not only to their own ark or corral, but also to other players' arks and corrals. Completing a part of another player's ark, giving another player a pair for his corral or ark, or completing a pair in a player's corral is considered a "good deed". For each one the player collects a "good deed" card good for points at the end of the game. Good deed cards can also be used to cancel "misfortune" cards drawn from the deck.

The object of the game is to collect matching cards that tell familiar Bible stories, including Noah's Ark, David & Goliath, Joseph and the Coat of Many Colors, Jesus in the Temple, and others.  The player completing the most stories is the winner.

Two of each picture are used for younger children, four of each picture for younger children.  The only distinguishing feature from Go Fish is an optional rule where the set collector must tell the Bible Story pictured before counting the set.

Features "Precious Moments" artwork on the cards.

A path with spaces of assorted colors is assembled by color matching the edges of large cards.  The players then take turns moving a single pooh figure with a color-coded spinner.  If Pooh is on a space with a printed honey pot, the player takes a cardboard honey pot counter.  When Pooh reaches the end of the path, the player with the most counters is the winner.

Pictionary with clay.  One team member sculpts with clay, the other team members guess what it is, with a one minute time limit.  Successes allow a die roll and another turn, failure passes control to the next team.  The categories are "Easy", "Medium", "Hard", and "All Sculpt".
This is a wargame on the Fall of South Vietnam and covers the last month of the war in 1975.
DTP forerunner of Operation Badr that West End published in 1984, remains the best Golan war game.

A board game for 2-4 players. Players move around the board buying up porn related businesses and managing hookers in a bid to be the first player to save up enough money to win the game. Porn industry businesses are divided into seven sub groups where players may attain a monopoly and gain make money even faster.

Collect Fate Cards as you go and gain benefits or the ability to play whammy events against other players. And watch out for the cops, else they bust your hookers and shut down your illegally run businesses.

Porn Masters is a light-hearted game and not meant to be taken too seriously. But don't let that fool you, there is still plenty of strategy involved.

With Action Baseball, you are the manager, coordinating your team's action against your opponent - batting, strategic plays, pitching, defense, and counter-strategies!  Your decisions direct the game flow, play-by-play, inning-by-inning.

Action Baseball is an original, two-person interactive card game with play dynamics true to the integrity and spirit of baseball.  No random dice.  No restrictive charts.  Instead, you determine the action!

Enjoy the rich tradition and excitement of baseball anytime, anywhere with Action Baseball!

two play decks
rule book

Gay-themed Monopoly variant published in a limited edition.

With Homonopolis you'll be bar-hopping in Amsterdam and be visiting the various gay venues. Different things will happen on the way, not only brought to you by the Social Service cards and Now What!? cards, but also by your own ingenuity in negotiations with your fellow players.

You can buy bars and other properties when you land on them. Note that you can only start to buy when you have run the total board at least once! Selling and negotiating is only allowed when it is your turn and before you have thrown the dice. Players are obliged to follow instructions such as Now What!? and Go To SM-Cellar.

This simple track game is from the Sesame Street "My First Games" series. The board has 4 tracks leading to an inner Finish Circle. Players take a colored monster pawn and place it on the Start space for its track. On your turn you spin the Cookie Monster spinner and move the monster (not necessarily yours) whose color was spun 1 space on its path. The players who own the first and last monsters to enter the Finish Circle are the winners.

The third extension to Alhambra called Die Stunde der Diebe (The Hour of the Thieves) is a collection of four separate extension modules that can be mixed with the base game in any combination (like in Die Gunst des Wesirs).

Module "Change" introduces coins into the game. When overpaying for a building, players now get partial change back in coins, which you can use as normal money cards.

With the module "The Thieves," thief cards are dealt to players in the beginning. The thieves allow players to take available money cards out of turn.

With the module "The Walls," wooden walls become available. Players can extend their city walls or close existing holes with them. To build walls, a player must first get an appropriate wall card from the money deck.

With the module "The Traveling Merchant," buildings in Alhambra begin to get populated by meeples! The new residents give extra points during the scoring rounds. To get the residents, players must do a little planning ahead with their city layout by using merchant tiles.

Expands:

    Alhambra

description:
4x4 (aka Vier mal Vier) is an abstract 4-in-a-row variant in which 2 players use 16 grooved cubes to erect a wall between them, aiming to be the first one to create a sequence of 4 identical shapes in their own side of a wall, or of all 4 different ones. This row can be horizontally, vertically, or diagonally. The cubical building bricks are all unique, come in 4 colours, and the only placement restriction is that each colour is only allowed once in each level or column. When the last brick is added, the completed wall will be 4x4.

On 2 opposing faces of each cube, a groove has been carved out. There are 4 distinct grooves, and the twist is that these grooves rarely mirror each other on either side of a cube. Therefor the wall face will look completely different to each player. The grooves are paired in such a way that within each group of 4 identically coloured cubes, only one cube has an identical shape on both sides (a different one for each colour). The other combinations are unique for that colour, and each combo occurs only one other time within the entire set.

At the start of the game, the cubes are placed on coloured supply strips which show the 4 'footprints' of all the blocks that exist for each colour. The wall is build on the table surface between the players. Players can only see the top cube of each column and the shape of the groove on their side of the wall of the cubes below it. Forward planning, memory skills and deduction all come into play to some extent, as players above all need to prevent that the opponent gets the sequence of 4 first. The supply strips serve as gentle reminders of what is out there.

The pro-version introduces the rule that an opponent can force a player to take back the last played cube if it would prevent the wall from completion, and instead insist on the placement of a cube that would result in a wall with the maximum amount of bricks. It also suggest the removal of the reminder strips.

[Not to be confused with the Wargames Club game Chronicle of the Three Kingdoms ]

This is a Japanese area movement wargame set during Romance of The Three kingdoms period of China. This wargame uses cards to supplement its area movement system.

Very rare even in Japan.

This game is re-development of Seikiheikuu.
family: Theme: Romance of the Three Kingdoms

description: This card game combines a Scooby-Doo theme with Blackjack-like mechanics. Players are trying to capture monsters. There are 12 Monster Cards (with high numbers) depicting various creatures from the TV show, and 40 Number Cards (with low numbers) depicting Fred, Daphne, Velma, Shaggy and Scooby. To start, a Monster Card is drawn and placed in the center of the table. On your turn you may either draw 1 Number Card from the pile or pass. You are trying to draw cards whose total is closest to the number on the Monster Card without going over. When all players have passed, the cards are revealed. The player who is closest collects the monster and draws first in the next round. The player who captures the most monsters wins.

boardgame: Mike Mulligan and His Steam Shovel Opposites Game
description:
"The object of all three games [Beginner's, Advanced, and Memory] is to match two puzzle pieces -- opposites -- and fit them together to make a pair.  Need help?  Each puzzle piece is specially cut so it only fits one piece -- the opposite that matches it.  That's why we call this a self-correcting game.  You don't need a grown-up to tell you if it's right.  If the pieces don't fit, you know you need to try again!"

Based on the book by Virginia Lee Burton.

description: The game comes with two 11x17" map, 280 counters, rules. The map scale is about 1.5 miles per hex. Each combat unit is one platoon to battalion. Turns are 8 hrs. The game simulates the opening few days of the dramatic Egyptian assault across the Suez Canal in October, 1973. The game includes an air game system, a ground system and unique rules where command and control are critical for movement purposes. Use of SAM missiles and SAGGER AT play important roles to keep the Israeli at bay. At first, it appears as a "David vs Goliath" battle because of the disparity of forces. The Egyptian needs to create breaches and bridging and move quickly before the balance favors the Israeli. Time is not a friend to either side at various times in the game.

boardgame: The Berenstain Bears: Join the Team
description:
This game contains 48 cards (12 4-card sets) with pictures of the Berenstain Bears playing various sports like baseball, ice skating and golf. Three games may be played...

The Winning Team: Four cards are dealt to each player and face-up on the table. On your turn, if you can match a card from your hand to a card on the table, you take both cards and place them face-up in a pile in front of you. If you can match a card to the top card on an opponent's pile, you may take the player's entire pile of cards. When no more plays can be made, the player with the most cards wins.

Sport Snap: The deck is dealt out evenly among the players. All players simultaneously turn over their top card. If no cards match, turn over another card. If two or more cards match, the first player to call out a correct matching sport wins all stacks with that card on top. If you make a wrong call, you must give 1 card to each opponent. The player that wins all the cards wins the game.

Spoons: Spoons are placed in the center of the table (one less than the number of players). Each player gets 4 cards. The dealer draws a card and either keeps it or passes it to the next player who also can either keep it or pass it etc. As soon as you have a complete set of 4 cards, take a spoon. All players then grab for the remaining spoons. The player who doesn't get one receives a letter - first an "S", followed by a "P", "O", "O", and finally an "N". When one player has spelled out SPOON the game ends and the player with the least number of letters wins.

The object of this track game is to be the first player to move each of his or her 6 space ships from Earth to another planet. On your turn you spin the spinner and advance a ship the number of spaces indicated. When you reach a planet, you must "orbit" it by following the track arrows around it. If you land on a space marked with a red triangle, you may leave orbit and head for another planet through the connecting path. If you stop on a space with a ship pictured, you may land on the planet. If you land on an opponent's ship, you send that ship back to earth. The first player to land all 6 of his or her ships on 6 different planets is the winner.

The goal of Energie 21 - Mit Energie in die Zukunft (Energy 21 - With Energy into the Future) is to produce as much energy as possible from renewable resources. Each player starts with a county that produces energy from coal, oil and nuclear plants including complete with factories, office buildings, residential areas and stores. All of these consume energy that has to be supplied. In the game it is not only important to plan and build carefully, but also work together with other players to realize large projects more effective through cooperation. Players can trade and haggle with power plants, victory points or leftover energy, but beware of the environmental assessment.
The game was designed for a German government agency to raise awareness of renewable energy planning on a communal level.

Based on the ground-breaking (at the time) Laserdisc-based videogame.

Race through the castle and rescue Princess Daphne.

Along with the standard die and cards to randomize the game, the board is also designed to shift. One layer is stationary, the other is mobile.

Race to obtain the Jeep and/or the Helicopter, then rush to the Finish space under the Tent to receive their transfer orders. The first player to reach the Tent with the Jeep or Helicopter (or both) gets a transfer home and wins the game.

Be the first player to capture three monsters, then return to Earth.

The game characters and artwork are based on the Battle of the Planets (Gatchaman) cartoon series from the same time period.

In the television market where I lived as a child (Western Massachusetts, Northern Connecticut, USA), this show was known as G-Force (a re-worked series, which put back the missing content from Gatchaman, but included a dreadful back-beat in every scene).

Description from MNG-AJM Games and Collectibles
Oct 2005 Catalog

TV and film related, licensed by King Features, who made the Popeye cartoons and the old serials that always ended at a crisis point. A game set on the Moons of Mongo. Each player takes the part of Flash, Dale, Zarkov or Emperor Ming. The board shows orbits around each of 6 moons and the players try to be the first to land spaceships on any four of these.  Movement is by dice roll, but you can choose which of your 4 ships to move.  Landing on an opponent's ship causes it to have to restart.  Orbits intersect giving quite a bit of choice of where to move.

Move your three pieces into position on the moons of Mongo. The first player to occupy three moons wins.

From Waddington ad dated 1976: "Flash Gordon Space Adventure Game - Your mission in this thrills-a-second game is to gain control of the moons of Mongo! Who will win? Flash Gordon? Dr Zarkov? Dale Arden? Or Ming the Merciless? Hours of excitement has been packed into this space thriller!!
Compete at placing bids on various records, with each player each using a deck of pre-set bids. The player with the records having the greatest point value at the end is the winner.
Players attempt to make six jumps in order to gather the most Combat Jump Cards. While the game uses a spinner instead of a die, the mechanism is purely "roll-and-move".

Players attempt to establish control of five consecutive Installation spaces on a Master Intelligence Chart.  These consecutive spaces may be horizontal, vertical, or diagonal. The ability to place Assistant Discs, however, is driven both by movement on the main board area and by successful dueling on the special Dueling Mechanism randomizer.

boardgame: Ali Baba and the Forty Thieves
description: Players compete at completing the adventures of Ali Baba. Players must collect a Prize from each of the Adventure areas. Each prize symbolizes one of Ali Baba's adventures in the actual tale. The first player to land by exact count on the Finish space with all four Prizes is the winner and is awarded the "Wedding of Ali Baba and Morgianna" token.

boardgame: Leave It To Beaver Money Maker Game

description: This "game" actually consists of a box full of 300 activities, divided up into low, semi, and high action levels.  The low action level activities may be questions, creative activities, or team-building exercises, while the high action level activities may consist of races or other athletic or dexterity games suitable for pre-adolescent parties.

boardgame: Girls' Nite Out: The Party Game for Women
description:
From the manufacturer:

Girls Night Out: The Party Game for Women is perfect for when the gals get together. This Sassi Games product is a hilarious game of conversation that gets all of those long-forgotten stories out. The game includes appetizer recipes, 10 funky cocktail recipes (for a charades game), scenario cards that prompt you to tell a story, score sheets, and hot pink pencils!!

From the publisher - ÆTHERVERSE: The Infinite Game of Miniature Battles Now available from Triskele Game Design Studios, Ætherverse is a science-fantasy miniature wargame with infinite possibilities. It's set in a sci-fi universe where the theory of multiple universes was proven to be true... and proving it set off wars across the Ætherverse by opening up new avenues of conquest. This backdrop provides limitless freedom to create worlds and armies, and to fight out wars between universes on your tabletop.

Ætherverse includes background material on over a dozen of these universes and the dominant empires on the Earth in each. The core rulebook includes 17 army lists, featuring the armies of these nations and races. Fifteen battle scenarios are also included to give you reasons to fight.

One of the key features of Ætherverse is the Army Creation System. Designed to allow the player to field any army they can possibly imagine, the Army Creation System presents a set of rules for creating an army from complete scratch. Design an army list to feature any miniatures in your collection and to play battles from anywhere within your imagination. Ætherverse gives you this freedom. There is no limit on what you can create

description:
From the back of the box:

Who is the film buff and who knows most about beer? Who is the sports freak of the party and who is Mr(s). Know-it-all? Maybe there's a talented sketch-artist or actor in the room. If you are blessed with all these talents, or you are just plain lucky, you stand a high chance to become 'Master of the Game'.

Play the Grolsch Master Game and test yourself. Play, roll the dice and become 'Master of the Game'.

Aim of the Game:
Try to reach the finish first with your swing-top bottle, pay the bill and win the game.

Gameplay synopsis With your swing-top bottle you travel from start to finish. Along the way you need to answer various questions or fulfill assignments. But watch out! Every ring brings you closer to the finish, but the questions and assignments become more difficult too. You go from one ring to the next via the doorman ar an open door. But... you don't reach the finish that easily. First you will need to make sure that you have paid your bill. With the beer voucher you won, you can pay the innkeeper. You win these vouchers by correctly answering the questions or fulfilling the assignments.

The Grolsch Master game is a promotional game from the Dutch brewery Grolsch, world famous (in the Netherlands) for it's swing-top bottles. The game is a party game that combines aspects of Trivial Pursuit, Pictionary, Charades etc.

The Trivia questions are divided into the categories Film, Beer, Sports and Various, each category containing 50 cards of 4 questions with increasing difficulty. The question you are asked depends on the ring you are in, the closer to the center, the more difficult the question should be. There are also 150 'the Pub' cards with 4 assignments that require you to draw, impersonate, act out or imitate persons, animals situations or even your thoughts on a particular matter (that you have written down beforehand). A sixth set of cards (30 brewery cards) are comparable to the 'Chance' cards of Monopoly.

You roll the die to determine the number of steps you have to take (you can decide whether you travel clockwise or counter clockwise), and depending on the square you land upon, you need to answer a question or carry out an assignment, you can get to the next ring or you get to draw a 'Brewery' (Chance) card. If you answer a question correctly, you get a beer voucher, otherwise you have to pay a beer voucher. If one of the other players guesses your assignment correctly, both get a beer voucher.

There a two possibilities to get to the next ring: via an open door, or via a doorman. The doorman requires payment in the form of beer vouchers. When you reach the final ring, you need to have enough beer vouchers to pay your bill. The first player reaching the inner circle wins the game.

description:
This picture matching game, with a Winnie the Pooh theme, is actually two games-in-one...
 Pooh's Birthday Party: In this game players try to gather up Pooh's friends for the party. On your turn you spin the spinner, place your finger on the same color Dialer hole indicated, and turn the Dial until your finger falls through the hole by Kanga & Roo. If the pointer points to Piglet, Eeyore, Tigger or Kanga and Roo, you may collect the piece of your chosen color that matches that character (if you haven't already). If it points to Pooh, you may collect the Pooh pawn. Be the first to collect the Pooh Pawn and four character pieces of your color to win. The winner gets to assemble all the character pieces to form the gameboard for Game 2.
 The Path to Tigger's House: In this game players once again spin the spinner and rotate the color dial, but this time they look at the gift that the character is holding. They then move the Pooh pawn along the gameboard path to the next space with the matching gift picture. The winner is the player that moves the pawn to the last honey pot space by Tigger's house.

The Blue Max is a Tactical level World War I Aviation-Flight themed board-wargame.

Japanese edition of both Jim Hind's Aces High/Blue Max combined, published  by Hobby Japan, with a beautiful map, photographing a miniature terrain. Also includes nice counters for nearly every model of aircraft which was used by each belligerant nations on all fronts.
