description: The game of Gung Ho is played on an 11 x 11 grid of points.  The playing pieces are plastic "towers" that are inserted into the grid points.  The idea of the game is to line up a fixed number of towers into a row either diagonally/vertically/or horizontally (5 for 2 players, or 4 for 3-4 players).  The game is quite simple, players take turns placing a tower piece into a game board location (once placed it cannot be moved).  The first player to meet the victory condition is the winner.

The game of Checards is played on essentially a checker board but with cardboard tiles representing playing cards.  One player takes the black suits and one the red suits.  Once the cards are divided, they are mixed face down and placed onto the playing board so that neither player knows the value of any of the cards until they are placed face up one at a time on the board.  Play proceeds by each player in turn moving a card piece forward in a diagonally direction one space.  The idea is to eliminate all the opponents cards (cards are eliminated by "jumping" them, but a card is only eliminated if the jumping card is of higher or equal value than the card being jumped)

Whoever holds the largest holdings gets the highest profits. But the cartel tags along and destroys some deals while others collect the big subsidies. This is Business!

Players place chips on the board to try to have the most of them on the valuable parts of the board. After 7 rounds, all chips are placed, and the game ends. Now each player receives points for their placed chips. The player with the most points wins.

Originally published in spielbox magazine.

National Games Inc #5014. Children's Nursery Rhyme game from the 1950's.  Players take turns rolling the dice then moving their pawn on the illustrated playing board following the instructions on the spaces that contain a rhyme.

From the box:

You are a special United Nations diplomat representing one of the five continents.  Out of the blue, a planetary hazard looms and only swift strategic action will avert it.  There are two races against time.  First you must use your cunning and tactical diplomacy to outwit your opponents.  In the meantime, however, the U.N. Special Envoy is following their course to save the world!

You must fly across the world in your specially commandeered aircraft, collecting and using solutions.  You may have to cooperate with other diplomats, who may also have their own hidden agendas.

Meanwhile, the U.N. Special Envoy moves towards their destination and it is out of your control.  Use your powers wisely.

Climb the Needle is "the space age game of the Seattle World's Fair 1962".  The gameboard is a graphic of the Seattle Space Needle.  Gameplay involves rolling 2 d6, moving the player pawn the lower of the two die rolls.  Rolling doubles allows the player to make an additional roll and landing on another player's space requires the player occupying the space to move back 5 spaces.

The Mission is an exciting new Christian collectible card game based on modern Christian life with all its challenges and rewards. Players take part in The Mission of life as they reach out to the lost, pursue their relationship with God and guard themselves against the works of the enemy.

Players try to move their 2 peanuts through the peanut butter factory while avoiding the Peanut Patrol Truck. On your turn you spin the spinner and check the result. If you spin a color, you advance one of your peanuts along the path to the next space of that color. If you spin "Hop to Front!", you move either of your peanuts in front of the leading peanut. If you spin "On", you start the Peanut Patrol Truck. This is an electronic truck that drives around a track, scoops up peanuts within its reach, and dumps them back into the START barrel. The first player to get both of his or her peanuts through the factory and onto the FINISH space wins.

This game features an electronic merry-go-round on a plastic faux iceberg. Two slides (one for each player) are aimed at its sides. Each player starts with 3 penguins. At the count of three, each player releases a penguin at the top of their slide where they wattle down and (hopefully) land in a merry-go-round slot, like a ball in a roulette wheel. If your timing is off, or if the slot is already occupied, you penguin will drain down a side-chute and back to your launch area. The first player to land all of his or her penguins safely onto the merry-go-round wins.

Judah the Christian Soldier Game's object is to complete the picture of Judah the Christian Soldier by placing armor cards in the correct location on the game board.  
This is done by obtaining game pieces by flicking the spinner, and if it lands on a piece of the armor that you do not have, you get it.

The game is simple and easy to learn.  The premise of the game is to help teach children about the spiritual armor of God that is referred to in the book of Ephesians 6:10-18 in the Bible.

The Book Game for Kids is a game based on The Book and The Book for Children paraphrasing the Bible.

The game itself starts by players picking what level of questions that they want to answer; Easy, Medium, or Hard.

The players have pawns that they move around the board from a Start point to a Finish line.  First player to cross the Finish line is the winner.  Along the way there are ladders and ropes, chutes, action arrows, and different square spaces that all have the players take certain actions.

The rules are able to be modified for younger players.  Older children will find the hard level questions on the Question cards to be a challenge though.  A good knowledge of the Bible, or Bible stories is a large plus for this game.  Scripture references are given on the cards too.

There are also Action cards that will give the player either rewards to help them along or setbacks that will hinder the journey along the board.

The books the game is based around (The Book and The Book for Children) aren't translated version of the Bible, but are easier to read the paraphrasing of the translations to help make the passages and stories easier to understand.
family: Versions & Editions: Junior Versions of Grown-Up Games

description: U.S.A. Trivia is a straight-up trivia game featuring United States geography, history and government.  Players move on a path looping through the 50 states, beginning in Maine and ending in Hawaii.  The color of the state the pawn is resting on determines the color used on the card.  Successful answers allow a die roll and another turn, unsuccessful answers pass control.

description: This is a simple matching game with a candy store theme. To begin, the 36 candy cards (gumdrops, candy corn, lollipops etc.) are randomly inserted into slots on the board. On your turn you spin the spinner and get either a result of "1 cent" or "2 cents". If you spin a 1, you pull 1 card from a slot. If you don't have a card in front of you, place it face up in front of you. If you do have a candy card in front of you, but this card doesn't match it, put it back (and remember what it is for later). If you do have a candy card in front of you and this card matches it, set both cards aside and draw again. A spin of 2 allows you to draw 2 cards on your turn. The player with the most matches when all the candy cards have been drawn from the board wins.

This children's track game is based on the Disney movie. To begin, puzzle pieces are placed face down in the center of Never Land, cards are placed on the Pirate ship, and players choose a pawn with a picture of a character from the film. On your turn you roll the die and advance your pawn around the Never Land path the number of spaces indicated. If you land on a Peter Pan space, you draw a card from the Pirate ship and follow the directions. If you land on a space with a picture of the character on your pawn, you draw a puzzle piece from the center of the board. If the color outline on the piece matches the color of your pawn, you keep it. When you have collected all 5 of your puzzle pieces you may move onto the boat track and head for HOME. The first player to complete his or her puzzle and reach HOME by exact count wins.

description: This is a simple track game based on the classic children's story. The gameboard depicts Mr. McGregor's garden. Vegetable chips are mixed and placed near the board (number side down), and the Mr. McGregor pawn is placed on a random garden space. On your turn you turn over a Vegetable chip and advance your Peter Rabbit pawn along the garden path the number of spaces shown on the bottom of the chip. If you land on a "bad" space, you lose your next turn. If you land on the Mr. McGregor pawn, you move backwards to the nearest "bad" space. After you move, you place the Mr. McGregor piece on any "good" space (hoping to block an opponent). Vegetable chips are removed as they are used so it's possible to deduce which move numbers remain. The first player to make it through the garden and reach THE END wins.

This is a wargame set during the German invasion of Poland in 1939. In addition to the historical scenario, it has a number of hypothetical changes that can be made to each side's forces. The game is primarily for two players, but it is possible to play with a third player controlling the Soviet forces.

The game was originally published in 1973, with a revised version published in 1974 and a further revision published in 1976.

description: This game was created to encourage players to find their own hidden spiritual self and to illuminate our understanding of ourselves in relationship to others and to the universe. The first section of the game involves questions of a Physical, Mental, and Spiritual nature. Further play reveals Insight questions, which elicit unexpected answers, and ultimately challenge each player to overcome self-deception and gain true insight. In answering the questions, players discover personal revelations which facilitate positive change and give rise to new visions for the future.

In this children's matching game players race to match all the cards of their color to items on their boards. Each player receives 2 boards depicting a Sesame Street scene with lots of characters and items. The item cards are mixed and placed face down within easy reach of both players. At the count of 3, each player simultaneously turns over a card. If the card's color is the same as the player's boards the player looks for the location of the item, shouts "I found it!" once discovered, and places it in a card space on the board's top. Players return cards that belong to an opponent. The first player to fill all eight card spaces on both of his or her game boards wins.

This track game, based on the theme park ride, is part of the Disneyland series of games. On your turn you roll the die and move along the track the number of spaces indicated. You may try to get a Treasure card by moving onto one of the branch tracks leading to a pirate holding a treasure chest. If you roll a number printed on the chest you take a card. Some cards show gold and are needed to win, others show Tiny (the fat lady at the auction) or a pirate in the dungeon and are worthless but are kept so that opponents don't know how many treasures you have. Landing on a peg leg pirate space forces you to lose a treasure. The first player with 2 treasures to reach the end of the boat track by exact count wins.

A simple game, a little like Snakes and Ladders. Not politically correct now. You race your coloured (and named!) donkey up the game board from 1 to 100. The board portrays a donkey being chased by a stereo-typed Black Maid.

No skill whatever required, progress is purely random. Slightly notable for the dice; there are four, each of which has the name of 2 of the donkeys twice and two of them once, so each donkey name occurs 6 times over the four dice.

Hoppit!  Is a Halma variant where each player has 6 toadstools and 5 frogs.  Toadstools move like a chess queen, frogs move by hopping over adjacent toadstools of any color.

The objective is to be the first to get all of your frogs to the opposite end of the board.

Contents:
6 toadstools in each of 4 colors
5 frogs in each of 4 colors
13 x 13 board

Insider Trading, which has been dubbed Pacific Northwest Rails II, focuses on the five major lines that served Spokane and surrounding vicinity.  It is the first Gandy Dancer game featuring the hauling of commodities.

As in the prior two Gandy Dancer releases, players earn money by making runs with their railroads and by buying and selling shares on the stock market. Players take turns investing in railroads and using those railroads to ship goods in this acquisition race.

Insider Trading has the added features of hauling commodities for additional profits, and as its name indicates, manipulating the stock market by using insider knowledge.  But don't get caught up in a scandal or it will cost you dearly.

Players start with a stock portfolio of $100 and some cash (the amount varies depending upon the number of players). In turn, a player may play a movement card, roll a die or pay to move around the gameboard. The space on which a player lands may allow that player to buy and stock in a railroad, use that railroad for shipping, draw and trade commodity cards (sets of which can result in big rewards) and draw insider trading cards (these allow stock manipulation). There are penalty spaces but these can only be landed on because of a die roll.

The game ends when one railroad has hauled one load of each of six commodity groups or when one player goes bankrupt. The player with the most money is the winner.

From an article on CNN.com:

The idea behind "Can You Beat Ken?" is a match up between Ken [Jennings, Jeopardy! champion] and the players as they try to answer some 1,200 trivia questions broken up into several different categories such as sports, science, contemporary music and history.

The challenge is to see which player, including Ken, is able to reach $2.6 million (the amount Jennings actually won on Jeopardy!) in prize money first.

"The game is unique in that we actually tested Ken with all 1,200 questions," [University Games spokesman Alex] Plant said, although neither he nor Jennings could recall how many of the game's questions Jennings actually answered correctly.

"Ken may have gotten some right and some wrong and we recorded how he answered each question on the card itself," Plant said. That allows players to see how they held up against the Jeopardy! whiz.

Pillage!  A game where you must pillage jewels from neighboring castles and make it back to your own before the other knights do.  You can be sent back to your castle without any jewels if you get caught or jousted with and lose.  
There are many paths you can take but some could be blocked.  The cool thing is that you have 2 knights to control so if one is at a road block or staying back to defend your castle, the other knight can continue the pillaging.
There are 2 dice involved: A specialty one and a regular 6 sided.  The closer to 4 players you have, the more interesting it gets with so many knight wandering around.

Set in an imaginary galaxy, the game pits the Triton Empire against the Megan Confederation.  The basis for the conflict revolves around the shortage of a mineral called "galactium" used to power the propulsion systems of starships and vehicles.  The Triton empire has invaded the mining planet Abarathascan (renamed Triton II after the invasion by the Tritons) to gain control of the galactium supply.  The Megan Confederation will have none of that and hence the invasion of Triton.

The game is played on 3 separate 8-1/2" x 11" hex maps depicting the main mining centers of Triton II.  Basic wargame mechanics such as Zones of control, Combat results tables, Terrain Effects Tables, describe the basic gameplay.  Both sides use transport ships to move ground units between maps.  Production points are alloted at the beginning of gameplay, used by each side to purchase their forces.  All space craft movement over the planet surface is conducted in a simultaneous plot mechanism.  Bases on the planet surface can be used to repair damaged craft or vehicles.  Victory conditions are determined by the scenario and involve accumulating victory points obtained by occupying the planets resource centers.

This game, endorsed by Lucille Ball, is a nine-hole tabletop miniature golf course. The course is molded plastic, complete with trees and water hazards. Some holes have special obstacles such as a tunnel, bridge, and a waterwheel. Everything slopes down to the center where a small golfer stands. He is controlled from the side of the board using a knob to pivot him in the direction you want, and a lever that swings his club. On your turn you place a metal ball in the depression in front of the golfer, aim him at the hole you're playing and shoot the ball. If you miss, the ball will drain down the slope and back into the depression. The player who finishes the course in the fewest strokes wins.
This game can be called a variant of Backgammon. On the stones of the players are Gold coins which can be stolen. Goal of the game is to exit the board with as much stones and gold as possible.

Street Violence is a tabletop miniatures game.
The rulebook is free to download on the publisher website.

boardgame: Murder Mystery Party: A Murder on the Grill
description:
The "Murder Mystery Party" line of murder party games is the most extensive. Part of the Bepuzzled line was originally founded by Lombard Marketing, Inc. University Games acquired Bepuzzled in February 1999.

These games play just like other main-stream murder party games like How to Host a Murder, Evening of Murder, Mysteries by Vincent, or Milton Bradley's Life of the Party.

Somebody put a barbecue fork through car dealer Tom Dooley, and that somebody is at your dinner party

This is a  complete 1950's murder mystery that places your guests as the chief suspects. . Six to eight friends play roles with plenty of scripts and suggestions on how to ham it up. The set includes an audiotape guide, background booklets for each player, special clues (including mysterious checks and blackmail letters), and anything else you need for an evening of unpredictable fun. There are even party invitations and tasty recipes

The suspects include:

Bobby Sox

Chubby Cheddar

Johnny Angel

Tiara Diamond

Ivan Spudnick

Barbie Q

This game is based on a German expression which refers to someone who tends to exaggerate when telling a story.  Literally, the expression would say "Don't convert a midge (mosquito) into an elephant."  This is the aim of this game.  Whoever is the first to convert their midges into elephants wins the game.

<!-- Entry audited by (nsolitander) (13-08-2009) --!>

This game features characters from the Disney film racing canoes down a river. The board is two river pieces that connect at the ends. If the lead player moves off the front board, the rear board is disconnected and placed in front. On your turn you play as many cards from your hand as you want/can: Canoe Cards move you 1 space, Rock and Log cards are used as hazards to thwart your opponents, Paddle Cards let you escape a hazard. If you land on a Leaf Space you draw a Leaf Token. The first player to collect 3 Leaf Tokens and land on a Shore Space wins.

This is a simple children's memory game using characters from the Disney film. Thirty character picture tokens are inserted into into leaf tiles that are then placed face down in a 5x6 grid. On your turn you turn over a tile so that all players may see it. If the token revealed is Pocahontas, John Smith or Meeko, you remove and keep the token then replace the tile face down. If the tile reveals a token of any other character, a character you've already collected or is empty, you replace the tile and do nothing. The first player to collect 1 Pocahontas token, 1 John Smith Token and 1 Meeko token wins the game.

From the Manufacturer:

You can have a "green thumb," turn "blue in the face," or be "tickled pink," but don't get caught Red-Handed. From "blue jeans" to "blackjack," and from "Yellowstone" to the "Red Sea," Red-Handed is the game where every colorful clue gets you a shade closer to a win. Guess the answer on the game card and your opponents will be "green with envy" as you get to pick from the Score Card pile. Pick a Score Card and you're well on your way to a win! But pick a card with a red hand on it, and you've been caught "Red-Handed!" Luckily, you won't have to cut through any "red tape" to get back in the game. Be the first player to collect five (5) Score cards and you're the 'thumbs up' winner! So "roll out the red carpet" and sharpen your "gray matter" - you've just been "given a green light" to play Red-Handed!

boardgame: Mark Breslin's Yuk Yuk's Comedy Game
description:
From the box...

Laughter becomes easy as you compete to play 1 of the 5 Closer cards in your hand against the Opener line on the table. Only after putting your card in the red viewfinder will you find out how the Yuk Yuk's panel of judges scored your card. Use enough of the funny Closer lines and proceed around the board and enter Yuk Yuk's comedy hall of fame.

boardgame: Crusher Joe: Roleplay Game in CrusherJoe
description: This is another Crusher Joe game from Tsukuda hobbies but this time uses a magic realm type system.

A monopoly (from Greek monos μόνος (alone or single) + polein πωλεῖν (to sell)) exists when a specific person or enterprise is the only supplier of a particular commodity.

Monopoly is a roll-and-move game where players move around the game board buying or trading properties, developing their properties with houses and hotels. When opponents land on players owned property, the owning player collects rent from the landing player. Owning sets of properties increases the rent paid to the player who owns them, by the player who lands on the square. The ultimate goal is to have the most money and drive opponents into bankruptcy.

This is a game like Monopoly, but centers around Alton, IL.

This is a game like Monopoly, but centers around Affton, MO. Includes game board, fate & chamber cards, property cards, play money, green and red discs, 4 tokens and 2 dice, and original instructions.

(from the back of the box)
"Give him back that ticket!
Ever since September 1960, when the very first parking ticket was given to an unsuspecting Doctor on call.  It has been a global game not to get a ticket.
Now the game has come to life and you can "Give him back that ticket!"  Over thirty years later and the game is still being played on the streets.  Even if you have never had a parking ticket, Beat the Traffic Warden will appeal to all as a fun, spirited and enjoyable board game.  It is not a game of pure chance, you need skill, tactical risk taking and determination to beat the Traffic Warden.
A game that generates great excitement and may bring out the rebel in you!  It's a game we can all relate to.  Feel really good handing the tickets back to the Traffic Warden, or get a great buzz from playing the Warden and handing out tickets yourself.  Whatever side suits your personality.  Beat the Traffic Warden makes the impossible possible."

Functionally, this is a straight-forward roll-and-move Monopoly game, but with a player-controlled adversary to add extra interaction to the game.  The plastic bits are very nice.
This is a game of shipping on the Great Lakes, involving picking up and delivering loads for profits while avoiding the many dangers of traveling on the Great Lakes.

description:
Buy, sell and trade famous companies as you try to cash in on good advertising or favorable publicity by avoiding pitfalls like anti-trust, recession or taxes. Empire allows constant bargaining with other players and decision making that will have you second guessing yourself most of the time.

The final object of the game is to have the most money when the tax revolt occurs at the end of the game.

Based on the "I Spy" series of books, this game offers the players a chance to create their own "I Spy" riddles.  Each player is given two word cards and a fill-in-the-blank rhyme card.  The players race to complete the rhyme using the two word cards dealt.  Other words to complete the rhyme may be found on "I Spy" cards on the board or objects in the room.  Players move around the board based on the order in which they finished their rhymes.

This game is part of the 3-D Expanding Adventure Series of Pokemon games that can be played by themselves or combined with other games in the series. In this game Giovanni is holding the president of Silph Co. hostage and it's up to the players to free him. Using dice for movement, players move through the many levels of Silph Co. in order to battle other players, catch various Pokemon, and get more power. They also search for Warp spaces that will move them to different levels, and the Red Warp that will take them to the space next to Giovanni. The first player to find the right Warp Tile and defeat Giovanni's Pokemon wins.

This game is part of the 3-D Expanding Adventure Series of Pokemon games that can be played by themselves or combined with other games in the series. From the rulebook:

Fuji has the Poke Flute you need, but you have to battle your way to the top of the Pokemon Tower to get it! Roll the dice and move through the rooms. Collect cards, battle other players, and catch Pokemon along the way. But beware! Ghost Pokemon may attack you. When you get to the top, you have one last challenge ... be the first player to defeat Team Rocket! If you do--you win!

description:
Two warring juggernauts, the Boltian Star League and the Kuissian Empire, have finally reached a peace accord after their first wearisome conflict. Unfortunately, the appearance of the Terran Union in a critical system destroyed this delicate peace in an instant, sparking the Second War which would haunt the participants for years to come.

Included for the player is a complete set of histories, ships, and scenarios covering the First and Second Boltians and Kuissian Wars. description:
Objective:  To collect five neighborhood place cards depicting places shown on a neighborhood map.

How to play:  Each player selects a neighborhood map card and holds it in his or her hand.  The remaining map cards are set aside.

The dealer shuffles the neighborhood place cards and deals five cards face-down to each player.  The remaining cards in the deck are placed face-down in a pile.  The dealer draws the top card and lays it face-up to begin the discard pile.

The first player picks up a card from either the deck or the discard pile.  If the card matches an item on his or her neighborhood map card, the player keeps the card and discards an unwanted card.  If the card does not match, the player places it face-up on the discard pile.

Players take turns picking up and discarding cards.  When all cards have been drawn from the deck, the discard pile is reshuffled to start a new pile.

The first player to collect five of the places displayed on his or her neighborhood map card wins the game!

A phonics game!  Consonant blends.  Help the critters make a word!

"It's a game of making words.  Just match the critters to their ships!  Play alone or with up to 4 players.  Includes 4 game boards, 40 playing cards, and simple directions.

It's easy!  It's fun!  It builds important phonic skills.

"Mapping the Town is a simple, fun game that introduces the concept of map reading.  Understanding the relationships of numbers and letters, logical thinking and attention to detail are skills players will develop during play.  One side of the board can be played as a simple lotto game of matching picture cards to identical but smaller pictures on the board.  The flip side of the board is also a lotto game that requires matching the grid coordinate on the card to the grid references on the board."

Jetan, or Martian Chess, is a two-player abstract game played on a 10-by-10 board.  Each side has a set of twenty pieces of varying types, with each type having its own set of movement options.

The game is unlike traditional Chess in a few important respects: 
All pieces in Jetan have limited movement (i.e. the Chief moves three spaces in any direction or combination of directions).
One piece on each side (the Princess) cannot capture other pieces.
The conditions for a win or a draw (described below) are unusual.

"The game is won when a player places any of his pieces on the same square with his opponent's Princess, or when a Chief takes a Chief. It is drawn when a Chief is taken by any opposing piece other than the opposing Chief; or when both sides have been reduced to three pieces, or less, of equal value, and the game is not terminated in the following ten moves..." -- from Edgar Rice Burroughs' novel "The Chessmen of Mars".

boardgame: Star Trek: The Invasion of Klingon Empire
description:
This is one of Tsukuda Hobby's SF Series games, #9 in that series (the game is numbered HG-009-4000).  It bears the subtitle "The Game of Space War in Star Trek" in addition to the less generic "Invasion of Klingon Empire."

This is definitely a strategic game, given the 4-part mounted map showing the United Federation of Planets, the Klingon Empire (or a portion thereof), the Romulan Empire (ditto), and a large amount of space between these.  Production is clearly important, as there seem to be many rules sections covering factories.

The game definitely appears to have two sides, the Federation and the Klingons.  It gives the appearance of allowing either 2 or 4 players - the map has a line down the middle which would split it reasonably enough for this purpose.  Presumably two players split the Federation and the other side has a Klingon and a Romulan player.

The missions of the USS Enterprise are clearly of some importance to the game.  The green lines on the map appear to chart these (many of the systems have names familiar from the original TV series), there is a rules section labeled "ST Game Enterprise Mission Chart," and one of the scenarios is called "Missions of Enterplize" (I swear!).  Other missions include "la Brandy de Camus" and a campaign game.

boardgame: Indian Ocean Adventure: The Japanese Raids on Ceylon, 1942
description:
Indian Ocean Adventure, designed by Marc Miller and released in 1978 by Game Designers Workshop, simulates the Japanese raid on the British bases in Ceylon in early 1942 and uses the same basic concepts and rules used by the designer in his previous games, Coral Sea and Battle for Midway.  It includes special design features to simulate the fluidity of modern naval actions dominated by aircraft rather than surface gunfire, including turn segments decided by an initiative factor and local weather variations, so common in the Pacific and Indian Oceans. Each counter represents a single capital ship or about 8 aircraft. Complex features such as aircraft maintenance and efficiency of land airfields are represented in the game rules.

This is an unusual subject. There certainly aren't many games that contain Fulmars, Albacores and Walrus in the counter mix.

The game includes:
240 counters, printed on one side only 
A 22" x 28" mapsheets 
A Rule Book 
Player aids and charts 
A pad of combination Ship Status and Location Chart sheets

description:
The Way of the Warrior is a highly abstract game of conflict in medieval Japan.  Players take the part of opposing daimyos and attempt to either capture the other player's daimyo or assault the other player's stronghold and rescue the hostage they are holding.

Each player has an identical set of 16 cardboard pieces representing his daimyo, a ronin, a ninja, archers, etc.  Each type of piece has different abilities - they have different ranges, some can attack more than once on a turn, some can jump other pieces, etc.  On each turn a player draws a card and then rolls either one die (if a Karma, or event card was drawn) or two dice (otherwise) and can that many spaces in total, using any number of pieces.

In addition to the Karma cards there are Warrior cards.  Some of these can be used for special effects and others can be collected in sets to deploy the more powerful units on board, among other uses.
This early Milton Bradley game used a top which you spin in order to try and score points into different holes on the playing board.

Each player begins with 5 cards from a standard 52 card deck plus 2 jokers.  Draw a card, keep it or pass it to the player on your left.  If you pass it, you must draw and keep the next card.  You may then discard pairs of cards, with some discards having special powers (e.g. force other players to draw cards).  The round ends if any player discards all their cards or the deck runs out.  Score penalty points for cards remaining in hand.  The player with the fewest points at the end of five rounds wins.

El Paso was designed as a version of Give Me The Brain to be played with a standard card deck, and was first published by Dalmuti's Restaurant in 1998.

Scaventure instantly injects spontaneity and fun into any dinner party, corporate ice-breaker or weekend get-together. Anything can happen - from discovering what the soup of the day is at the local diner or dressing a male team member in drag to acquiring an undertaker's business card or acting out a scene from a classic movie. Ages 16 and up.

Scaventure Kids asks kids Ages 6 - 12 to locate items around the house or yard or even create, perform or draw new things themselves. Whether it's demonstrating a pet trick, making a sandwich a gorilla would like or learning to hula dance.

Editorial Review from Amazon.com:
"A scavenger hunt in a can, Scaventure Kids provides everything needed to plan and execute a challenging hunt. Included in the cardboard container are 150 cards, instructions, and team stickers. The cards each have four items for a team to find or do. Cards ask players to do things like "Find two bags with different store names" or "Have your team act out a scene from your favorite movie." Each team takes out cards and has 30 minutes to carry out the tasks. At the rendezvous, the teams share their finds and winners are chosen. For rainy days, vacations, or parties, Scaventure Kids is a simple and creative activity that fosters teamwork and creative problem-solving. --Lee Strucker"

Board game version of Noel Edmond's UK Trivia TV series "Telly Addicts."

Answer questions on specialist subject by moving around your chosen department - Soaps & Sagas, Top of the Pops, Guess Who, Comedy, Children's TV, Comedy, News or Sci-Fi.

Be the first player to get each of "your star tokens" back to the remote control. Only then can you take the Star Challenge and become champion Telly Addict.

A Question of Pop is the board game of the highly successful UK BBC television series hosted by Jamie Theakston.

Promises to keep your family and friends entertained for hours as they remember everything from the music they listened to many years ago through to more modern music featuring rounds such as A Side/B Side and Name the Year.

The mechanics include dice based movement and time limited question answering using an egg timer.

"A Question of Sport" is the trivia board game version of the amazingly popular UK BBC television series of the same name.

The original version of the game, featuring the TV game show hosts Bill Beaumont, David Coleman and the late lamented Emlyn Hughes on the box cover was released in 1986 and included 1200 question and answer cards, 2 scoreboards, 2 picture boards, 2 dice and 2 shakers.

It remains ever popular and has been re-released many times and in several formats since including junior and sports themed expansion versions.  Later versions of the game also included a "Mystery Guest Video Cassette."
family: TV Shows: A Question of Sport

description:
Questillo is a game of crossword building - each player has a grid on a sheet of paper, and adds words to it to make a crossword type layout.

The words added are based on clues, which come from cards drawn from a box.  So a clue might say 'A great game', and each player has to get the name of a great game added to their existing set of words on their grid.

Think of a cross between 'Pictionary' and 'Scrabble', and you're nearly there...

Points are achieved for words accepted by everybody, and can be doubled or tripled on certain bonus squares.  Most points wins!

War Stories is the first game by Symbiose, a small Dutch publisher. Only 1000 copies were printed.

It is a small strategic boardgame for two players that can be best described as a combination of Chess and Memory with a 16th century feudal Japan theme added to the mix.
The story is that two Daimyos are discussing a battle they once fought, during a tea ceremony (the discussion, not the battle). The problem is that both Daimyos don't have very good memories. They try to remember how the battle developed and who won eventually.

As mentioned, the basic mechanics of the game are a combination of Chess and Memory. Finding combinations of identical cards on the board results in new units, which act as chess pieces. Hence, good memory and some tactical insight may be of good use in playing this game.

Based on the UK Television Series of the same name following the exploits of the Manchester couch potato Royle family.

The object of the game is to answer trivia questions in order to collect Club biscuits.  You miss turns if "Dave buys you a pint in The Feathers" or if "Barbara puts the kettle on for a brew......."

The  mechanics are simple dice based movement with card drawing.  Three decks of cards are used: Television Facts, Chance cards and the endearingly titled "My Arse" deck which takes it's name from Jim Royle's favourite saying.

Believe it or not there is a special edition which comes with a novelty toilet seat cover with a picture of Jim Royle on it!

"My Arse" is a Cheatwell Games trivia game based on Granada Television Ltd's "Royle Family" situation comedy show.

Its a variation of the card based trivia theme in which players pit their wits against Jim, the Royle Family's fountain of all knowledge and have to decide if "facts" on the trivia cards are "Sound" (true) or "My Arse" (false.)

Here's how the marketing material describes it:

A classic game of truth or bluff, you have to choose the facts from the guff...decide whether Jim really knows his onions or is simply full of hot air.

Do you agree with Jim and head for a pint at the Feathers or grumble your way to the kitchen to brew another cuppa?

Of one thing you can be certain...you're in for a right Royle battle in this off-beat trivia board game.

A deck of cards marked with special functions as well as standard card values.  The game is played during a round of disc golf.  Each player receives three cards at the beginning of the game and one for winning a hole.  They may play their card to perform the action stated on it, such as taking a mulligan, subtracting one stroke from their score, trading a disc with another player, swapping 'lies' with another player, or forcing another play to throw with their off hand.  An official variant involves collecting the best poker hand by the end of the game.

description:
Dr. Futsch is Alex Randolph's version of Sleuth, a simple deduction cardgame.  The goal of the game is to find the infamous master of disguise, Dr. Futsch.  There are 8 distinct disguise items and Dr. Futsch randomly chooses 3 of them for his disguise.  Other players try to guess who Dr. Futsch is from 56 possible choices. 
One year later published as Disguise (same graphics).

Re-implemented by 
Das lustige Detektivspiel für Kinder

"Global Pursuit is a game of strategy and luck for those who enjoy learning about the world we live in. With four different levels of difficulty, it's also a game the whole family can play!

The object is to race your opponents around the world in your balloon, picking up your flags (in the correct order of course) by throwing the dice, answering questions and moving forward. 
First player to collect all seven flags is the winner."

Spider-Man is up against the evil Doc Ock in this exciting new game of racing and capturing. Every player takes on the role of Spider-Man jumping onto buildings and swinging along special spaces.
Get to the clock tower and attempt to rescue Jane from Doc Ock. Fail and you may go back to start. Succeed and you can race to the finish. Don't let Dock Ock thwart your attempts to succeed.

boardgame: Cyberpunk Episode I: The Battle For Cyber-dump
description:
From Tesera.ru:

An exciting board game with plastic models of cyborgs (walking) and zhucybers (crawling) that shoot. Real shooting on playing fields with variable terrain.

The game is about cyborg battles and gathering raw materials in the dystopian distant future. Stylish décor (a dim dump field lined with a variety of obstacles and containers), excellent miniatures of bipedal cyborgs and six-legged zhucybers. Real shooting with plastic shots, and the damaged parts of the cyborgs' bodies are removed, respectively limiting their capabilities; Thus, the increased firepower of bipedal modifications is compensated by reduced survivability. There are many different finds on the field, and cyborgs can also have different specializations, whether it's assault or, for example, electronic warfare. The games are quite fast and fun. Overall, a very good game

A list of components:

     Two-sheets 90x60 cm playfield
     88 play cards
     2 pools for play cards
     A set of bullets for shooting
     D6 playing die
     32 pages rulebook with legend story

"Only Fools and Horses" is a Monopoly style game based on the BBC TV comedy series of the same name.

You move around a monopoly style board encountering spaces or drawing "goods","chance", of "location" cards depending on where you land and gaining (or losing) money as you go.

French 2-player abstract game from Jacques Le Floc'h.

There are three types of counters in the game (triangles, hexagons and circles. These counters can be moved on the matching designs located on the board. They can also pile up and in that case their powers accrue. A triangle-hexagon can so move as a triangle and as a hexagon.

1 Hexagonal board
9 pieces per side (3 triangle, 3 hexagon, 3 circles).

From the box:
- Gain Territory
- Organise your troups for attack
- Assault the fortress of your opponent.

Contents:
- Game board
- 12 yellow pieces
- 12 red pieces

Capturing game in which the goal is to be the last player with pieces remaining.  Pieces move orthogonally only, changing direction after each step.  Capture is by replacement.

Contents:
- 1 board
- 12 pieces
- 1 die
Tactical combat between clay monsters you create.

description:
"Big Breakfast Vital Statistics"  is a card based trivia game with a difference.  It is based on the "Stats and Facts" feature of UK Channel 4's Big Breakfast TV show presented by Johnny Vaughan and Denise Van Outen

The game has very obscure trivia questions such as "that only 28 per cent of pet stick insects are call Sticky but 42 per cent are called George" or that "40 blind people are registered to drive cars in Palermo."

The aim is not to get the answers right, but to be closest to the correct answer.

The AADA Vehicle Guide, "2034 edition", contains 129 pre-made Car Wars vehicles in various classes. In addition, there are 133 optional configurations giving a total of 262 possible vehicles. The Guide also includes an index to Car Wars weapons and gadgets, hints on customizing vehicles, new rules for off-road combat, and new rules for three-wheeler vehicles.

Part of the Car Wars Series.

A set of simple instructions for fleet engagements in the age of sail.

From the Introduction section (page 2)"This game contains a number of novel mechanisms designed to capture the flavor of naval conflict in the great age of sail. We freely admit that they contain a touch or two of "Hollywood," but they should give you plenty of excitement, some doubts, uncertainties, stress and confusion. Record keeping is minimal and a fairly large fleet action can be fought in two or three hours."

Players are advised to furnish the following:
A handful of six-sided dice, some 'broadside fired' markers made of white cotton and some 'on fire' markers made of colored cotton. Three red and three black cards from an ordinary pack of playing cards. Finally, two fleets of 1/1200 or 1/3000 scale model ships.

- Damaged Steering
- Command and Control 
8) Wind Direction Spinner
9) Point of Sailing Indicator (player aid)
10) Broadside Indicator (arch of fire player aid)
11) Fallen Mast Indicator (player aid)

description:
The Game of Catch which was produced in Toronto, Ontario, Canada in th 1970s by the Arrco Playing Card Company.

Included in the flap-top box are instructions and information for parents in both English and French. The box is 9.2cm long x 6.cm wide x 1.5cm thick

Card backs contain drawings of sea creatures. The front of each of the 32 cards in the deck (8.9cm long x 5.8cm wide) contain drawings of fish, walrus, lobsters, crabs, whales, etc.. The name of each sea creature is printed on the bottom of each card. Colored dots related to the play of the game are in the corners of cards.

This game is currently part of the collection of the Games Museum at the University of Waterloo, in Waterloo Canada.

This game was manufactured in Paris, Ontario, Canada by the Walker Press, Ltd. in 1967 as one of the objects made available during the Canadian Centennial Celebration. The instructions and other text appears in both of Canada's official languages - English and French.

The flap-top box is 8.7cm long x 6cm wide x 1.3cm thick. The box includes the official Centennial Celebration symbol which can be seen in more detail on the back of each card.

There are 4 instruction cards in the deck which offer details for playing four different games with these cards. Each card is 8.5cm long x 5.5cm wide and features a date of note in Canada's history and a sketch of a person who played a role at that time in the history of Canada. Below the sketch are some historic details about the time period and the person's role. The various numbers and symbols on each card relate to playing the games.

This game is part of the collection of the Games Museum at the University of Waterloo in Waterloo, Canada.

This multi-faceted colorful bilingual game was issued without charge to schools and citizens who requested a copy from the Commissioner of Official Languages of the Canadian Federal Government's Language Development Program.

All of the components of the game fit into the cardboard game board, which when closed is a large envelope that is 35.7cm long x 31cm wide. The game was created by Madeline Knonby and illustrated by Geo Boka, copyrighted 1974.
The game board when open is 35.7cm long x 62.3cm wide and about 1cm thick, and is a stylized map of Canada. Each province and territory (of the time) is in a different color and major cities are indicated on the map appropriately. In addition there are colorful scenes depicting various activities across Canada. Some places are indicated with their names in English  and others indicated with their names in French. Different types of  lines between cities illustrate modes of transportation between cities. For example, the dotted black line at the bottom between Winnipeg and Regina indicates a railroad, while the double red lines from Winnipeg going east indicates a highway; the yellow line from Churchill going northwest indicates an air-route and the white and black line going northeast indicates a water-route! The "legend" at the lower-left side of the board indicates these transportation modes as well as moves of players during the game.

The spinner on the board indicates the transportation modes a player must use while attempting to travel across Canada. The 32 page included bilingual book contains instructions for playing the game and other bilingual information. The game is for four players (illustrated in circles on the front of the book), each of whom is represented during play by a 1.5cm diameter plastic disc of white, green, red, or yellow.

The 33 1/3 RPM phonograph recording (17.8cm diameter) includes 2 songs in both English and French. There is also a glossary of phrases in both languages which includes phrases such as: "It's snowing", etc.

This game appears to be a modified later version of an earlier game - From Coast To Coast, but an earlier version of The Great Game Of Canada.

Quick Draw McGraw was manufactured by Edu-Cards Manufacturing Corporation, New York City. There are 36 cards in the deck, each 9cm long x 5.8cm wide. Cards are numbered one through ten in the upper right and lower left corners. Each card has circular symbols in the center representing one of four "suits" and matching the number printed in the corners. The circular symbols are supposedly "bullet holes" - the result of McGraw's ability. The "Ace" or number one card features a sketch of "Quick Draw McGraw", with the words Lucky One printed across the top and bottom of the card.

Somewhat like snakes and ladders, the object of  Quick Draw McGraw is to move your characters and be the first to reach the ranch. Alternate paths provide detours or shortcuts, and movement is governed by a spinner. The players' pieces are characters from the TV Cartoon show. This game is not the same as the 1960 Quick Draw McGraw Private Eye game, which used a similar mechanic but a completely different board. 
Recommended Ages 5 - 10

Game of Rebellion was first produced by A. Williams and Company, Boston, Massachusetts (US) in 1861 with subsequent editions being produced by Heyer Brothers through the early 20th century. It was copyrighted by its inventor Henry A. Sampson.

The subject matter of the game is the American Civil War.

The game is for two or more players. There are 50 cards (5.8cm long x 9cm wide), and 7 "counters (1.7cm)". The cards are divided into 3 "suits" - red, blue, black - which have significance in the play of the game. On each card, the name of a prominent political or military personage of the time appears, or the name of a city which was the scene of a battle. The cards, counters, and instructions are encased in a box 11cm long x 7.6cm wide x 1.7cm high .

The Game Cards are divided evenly between “Union” and “Confederate” sides. The “Union” cards are printed in red ink and include a card marked “Washington” representing the Union Capitol along with 24 cards each with the name of a Union General, Officer, Politician or Ship. Included is a card for Abraham Lincoln, Colonel Ellsworth, the frigate Lawrence, Winfield Scott, General Sherman, General Butler and many other Union Officers. The “Confederate” cards are printed in black ink and include a card marked “Richmond” representing the Confederate Capitol along with 24 cards each with the name of a Confederate General, Officer, Politician or Ship. Included is a card for Jefferson Davis, Privateer Petrel, General Lee, General Johnson, General Beauregard and many other Confederate Officers.

The Game was played between two people – one took all the Union cards and the other the Confederate cards. They players would lay down the card representing the capitol of their respective countries and the box, containing 24 blue and one red token would be placed far enough away from the players so that they could not see the tokens inside. Play would begin with one player laying down a card at the center of the table and the other player choosing a card to “oppose” that card. Then the players would each take turns drawing a token from the box – when a player drew the single red token from the box he would “win” that battle and take his opponent’s card “as prisoner”. This play would continue until one of the players captured all of the original cards from the other thus being declared the “Victor”.

A Heyer Brothers edition game is part of the collection of the Games Museum in Waterloo, Canada.
Players score points by adding stars to their solar systems and hooking stars with star hooks.
Golf with a deck of special cards.

A strategy game where players are dealt hands of cards containing specific patterns, and a central trough of white and dark colored balls sits in the middle of the table between all players. Each player on their turn is allowed to lift one ball out of the tray and place it on either end of the line. Because the tray is slightly curved, all the remaining balls bunch together. Players try to win pattern cards by moving the wooden balls to match the card pattern.
Score as many points as possible by getting the cards that complete any list or lists found on the cards.
Build a railroad empire connecting farms, ranches, mills, & mines and earn more money than the other players.

boardgame: Dora the Explorer Scavenger Hunt Game
description:
Meant to be played individually or as teams, the game consists of 100 task cards each of which lists four items to acquire or tasks to perform.  Half of the cards are for indoors and half for outdoors scavenger hunts.  Tasks could be anything, such as the following:

- Bring back something that has a checkered pattern.
- Build a house out of Lego building blocks.
- Find a watering can.
- Stretch elastic bands over a board and try to play a song as if it were a guitar.
- Make up and draw a flag for the city or town you live in.

The game also includes player-task stickers with Dora the Explorer characters on them.

Ideal for parties or groups of children.  Adult participation encouraged.

From the box:
Read'em is a family game that combines the skill of word building with the thrill of poker. The object is to win chips from other players by creating the best "hand" or by bluffing your opponents into thinking you have the best hand! Can a clever wordsmith outplay a wily poker bluff? Who'll win all the chips? That's the fun and excitement of Read'em!

Contents of the box
The game comes with a deck of 54 letter cards, poker chips and the rules to play 3 different poker games. You get the rules for Five Card Draw, Seven Card Stud and Hold'em (Hold'em is based on Texas Hold'em).
Game of capturing your opponent's pieces by first placing your pieces strategically and then moving them strategically.

This one included a real world treasure hunt.

description: Six beautiful illustrated games boards introduced children to trades, numbers, fruits, vegetables, animals and miscellaneous items. Silhouettes on the back of the boards add a new dimension to the game, stimulating abstract thinking and perception. The aim of the game is to fill the game board with randomly selected cards.

boardgame: Sir Hinkle Funny-Duster, the Comical Game
description:
The game is played with a deck of 20 cards divided into four FAMILIES; The Funnyduster Family, The Butler's Family, The Huntsman's Family, and the Gardener's Family.  With 5 cards to each family, there are cards for the father, wife, son, daughter and the family symbol.

Cards are shuffled and dealt evenly to all players.  Each player in turn freely asks any player for a specific card, if that player has the card asked for, the player must give that card to the person asking.  Play continues for the asking player until the person asked does not have the card asked for and the turn goes to the next player.

Once a set of Family cards is collected it is laid on the table and retired from play (scoring no points for the player).  The object is to be the player that lays down the last Family set

A twist in the mechanics of the game adds fun to the gameplay.  Anytime a card is given to a player, the receiver must say "Thank You", if the receiver fails to do so, the first person to say "Duster" receives all of the cards from the person that neglected to say "Thank You".  The person receiving the cards must in turn say "Thank You" or is subject to the same "Duster" rule.

The person that did not say "Thank You" and lost all of their cards can not be spoken to.  They do not get any additional cards, but if someone speaks to this person, then the first person (any player) to say "Duster" receives the cards of the person that spoke to the person with no cards (and again, "Thank You" must follow receipt of the cards or the "Duster" rule applies again)

A very simple roll-and-move mechanic driven by a spinner propels cardboard stand-ups of the Munsters TV Show Characters around the track.

VERY rare and highly sought after by collectors.

An expansion for the Gear Krieg Wargame base game that extends the system to the North African Theater.  Includes:

- Alternate Historical Background for the Desert Conflict.

- Table Top Rules for fighting in the Harsh environment of Africa.

- 7 detailed desert scenarios with maps.

- Basic TO&E (Tables of Organization and Equipment) for the expeditionary forces of Germany, The British Commonwealth, the United States, Italy, and France.

- 50+ vehicles and variants from all nationalities.

Expands:

    Gear Krieg Wargame

"The Full Monty" is a boardgame based on the award winning film of the same name.

The winner of the game is the first player to get a job - but you may have to go the full monty before you finish the game!

First, ensure all the players have the same number of clothing items. Gather together a  selection of clothing, the more bizarre, the better and place them in a pile on the floor, this pile will be known as the kitty. Before you begin the game, decide your group's criteria for The Full Monty. Is it as it should be, totally naked, in the buff, or will you chicken out and go down to underwear only? The choice is yours!

Ad-mad is a mime and acting based game based in the wacky world of television advertising. The game is played on a board featuring eight "rooms" and each player has their own room and television remote control.

Cards that look like televisions invite the players to sing, mime or enact some of the most memorable and funniest product advertisements.

High standard antics are awarded with "buttons" for the remote control, and players move around the boards in search of a full set of buttons.

Top Gear Motorsport is one of several board games based on the popular BBC UK television series Top Gear hosted by Jeremy Clarkson.

The object of this racing trivia game is to be the first past the chequered flag at the end of an agreed number of between 1 & 11 laps.

Movement around the circuit depends on answering some pretty tough motorsport questions.  Get the answer right and your foot is on the accelerator, get it wrong and you are going backwards!

Though (motoring) trivia based there are many variables which can improve or reduce your chances of winning: The circuit itself is tortuous so keeping to the racing line is essential.  Hazard and weather cards, which can turn the track into a skid-pan make life interesting so choice of tires is critical, as is choice of fuel.

Game Contents:-
1 x playing board
1 x blue dice
1 x red dice
6 x coloured lap counters
6 x coloured racing helmets
388 x motorsport cards
12 x tyre cards
1 x set of red rocket fuel tokens
1 x set of blue fuel tokens
1 x lap counter board
1 x rules sheet

The box notes describe this game as a fast action game of skill, strategy, and racing knowledge.

You need a very good general knowledge of motorsport to enjoy this game to the full, but the rules include a shorter, faster version of the game can be played by using only the hazard cards and no questions, making game-play much less difficult.

This wargame is a part of the Micro Warfare Series covering World War I naval combat. It is a miniatures-style game that includes cardboard ship counters. The rules include both Small Action (individual ship command/gunnery) and Fleet Action (squadron command/gunnery) rules. Specific counters are included to fight the Goeben Incident, the Falkland Islands, Dogger Bank and the battle cruiser action at Jutland.

Supplements
Naval WWI
 TTG-MN-101 British Jutland Set
 TTG-MN-102 German Jutland Set
Move around on a pirate map, and collect as much treasure as possible.
Two teams compete in three events. Get the right ingredients for a specific dish, general questions on food and organize different products according to how nutritious they are.

From the box:
"What do you know of actors, Ferrari-cars, exotic destinations, movie-lines, champagne and everything else connected to a life in fame."

Based on a topic, such as Clint Eastwood or Stockholm, a number of statements about the topic in question are read out. As soon as you think that a false statement is read out, you shout out. If it's correct that the statement was false, you can go on the game board based on a roll of the dice and take home the amount of money you end up with. The player with the most money in the end wins.

Based on the BBC quiz programme "Mastermind", Magnus Magnusson presents the ultimate quiz with 10,000 questions.

There is no board or dice. The box with the questions in is simply passed from one player to the next each acting in turn as quizmaster.

The 10,000 questions are arranged into ten colour coded categories:

Famous People
Common Sense
History
The World
Stage and Screen
The Sciences
Sports and Games
The Arts
The Animal World
General Knowledge

The questions themselves have been designed to intrigue and arouse curiosity and so are interesting to ask. The answers are often unexpected, sometimes incredible, sometimes obvious.

The ultimate quiz game may be played by an unlimited number of people.

From the game website:

Sign It! the board game is an interactive new way of learning and practicing American Sign Language (ASL). It is designed to be fun and educational.

Sign It! was born out of necessity. The creator is a 50 year old cabinet maker with a progressive hearing loss. While taking ASL classes, he searched in vain for a teaching tool to help him and his wife practice ASL and increase their vocabularies, and subsequently developed Sign It! to fill this need.

The Sign it! board game which includes a board a rule booklet and markers for six players, provides instruction for learning 824 signs. The game also comes with a copy of "Signing Illustrated" :The complete learning Guide, a 258 page illustrated and indexed reference book.

Sign It! contains a set of cards (which cover more than eight categories), a rule booklet, one die and markers for six players.

The game is setup reminiscent of Trivial Pursuit, with color coded squares.Each person gets a token (I was the elephant) then rolls the die. As expected,what ever the number you rolled...that's the number of spaces you moved. The color of the square is the color you look at on the card. Then you sign that word.

Colors are categories such as feelings, communication, locations, and more.On the card next to the words are page numbers for the accompanying book where you can find the appropriate sign. The game can be purchased with the reference book, "Signing Illustrated" by Mickey Flodin, which includes well drawn illustration, clear directions and even little reminder hints to help you remember new signs.

description: Posto di Combattimento  is a little but complete set of wargame rules about Naval Tactical combats in World War 2, this 32-pages booklet contains also all the characteristics for naval and aircraft units for Germany, UK, Italy, France, Japan and USA.

description:
This is a family game where each player is a "verb" traveling though the mines of Solomon trying to find Solomon's Treasure.  King Solomon (from the Old Testament) was both a very wise and very wealthy king.  In this game you are both seeking his monetary treasure and learning from his treasure of wisdom as found in the book of Proverbs.

The game is for Ages 8 and up but includes rules for younger children.

boardgame: Quick Draw McGraw Private Eye Game
description:
"QUICK DRAW is trying to solve some of the mysteries that he has heard about. He needs the help of the players in this game to find the missing parts to each mystery. Each player will use one of QUICK DRAW'S friends, BLABBER, SNOOPER, BABA LOOEY, or DOGGIE DADDY to help him."

Players take turns spinning the spinner and moving around the board. The space they land on determines which pile they draw a card from. Players may challenge other players for cards, and even steal cards from other players' hands. The first player to collect all eight puzzle cards detailing their assigned mystery wins.

Spider-Man is a basic Memory game with a few twists.  First, there are 48 cards, including two 'Wild Cards' that may match with anything turned over.  Second, there are two pairs of 'trick destroyers' that take a trick away from a player.  Finally, there are 5 pairs of 'Take Spider Man' cards that allow a player to capture the Spider Man figure.

Spider Man is worth 4 Tricks at the end of the game.

Simple fantasy-themed abstract strategy game from the mid-80s, when such things weren't as popular as they are now (early 2000s). Published in Warlock (Issue 13 - Dec/Jan 1986/1987).

The board depicts the classic demon-summoning layout, with pentagram and magic circle.  One player is the wizard, the other, the demon; both are armed with tokens, two for each ancient element (earth, air, water, fire).  The wizard plays first, setting up his elements hidden at the points of the triangle and pentagram with no opposing element on the same line.  The demon plays second, laying down one element at a time next to the wizard's and seeing what happens before playing the next.  The idea is that the wizard should create a difficult-to-guess defense, while the demon tries to solve the puzzle and/or hedge his bets.

boardgame: The Addams Family: Find Uncle Fester! Card Game
description:
The search for Uncle Fester is on!

The object of this card game is to collect all seven members of the Addams Family: Gomez, Morticia, Lurch, Pugsley, Wednesday, Thing, and Uncle Fester. There is only one Uncle Fester card, which makes collection of a complete set difficult. You cannot win if you are holding the Tully Alford card (he's the devious family lawyer).

In addition, there is an action card deck, which instructs the players to ask for, search for, trade, and pass around cards. Recommended for Ages 8 and up.

Strategy Rummy is described as "a bet and bluff game." This game was published as a follow-up to another MB game: Strategy Poker.

Strategy Rummy is played using a vinyl table cloth, mini poker chips, and a special deck of cards (which includes six wild cards). Each player is dealt seven cards and everyone antes one chip into the pot. The sequence of play is as follows:

A. Draw a card
B. Discard a card
C. Place a meld or lay off cards onto the vinyl table cloth
D. Ask for bets from opponents.

Players go out by playing all seven cards from the hand onto the vinyl cloth. The player with the most chips wins the game.

     Heroes of Laur
