description:
(From the BrickMech site:)

BrickMech is a d6-based game where Mechs built of LEGO® bricks vie for ultimate control of the battlefield. Constructed in a unique modular system, Mechs can scavenge parts blown off other Mechs to improve their stats. The game is designed to be a rich gaming experience that's simple to play.
1946 game from Milton Bradley. A simple roll and move game. Also known as the Build a Happy Home Game.

Use game cards and board pieces with your Orient Expedition LEGO sets to overcome challenges, defeat villains and discover hidden treasure.  Each player controls a unique LEGO character, with specific speed, luck, and strength attributes.  Move around the board, collecting items to enhance your character's abilities so that you can overcome obstacles.  Combat LEGO villains that stand in your way.  The challenges and obstacles are essentially a scripted "Indiana Jones" type storyline.  When you have overcome the final challenge, all players win.  You can rearrange your game board and sets to create all-new adventures.

It has a yellow and black duo-toned drawing of Regan and Carter on the box, and claims to be 'an exciting game for four players'.

The game parts are: a board, a scoreboard, 16 small cards and 4 small plastic figures representing The Villains (typical Burgler Bill 'swag' type figures) and The Sweeney (Policeman blowing a whistle). Oh, and two dice.

The idea is that you work in pairs, two players are the villains, and two are The Sweeney. You have to meet up with your partner at randomly designated places on the board for a raid. When you successfully raid a location you get points to add to the scoreboard, and the winner is the first up the scoreboard to number 14.

boardgame: The Nags Head Board Game (Only Fools and Horses)
description:
Mange Tout Rodney, get down the apple & pears, get your cocktail umbrella & jellied eels ready 'cos you won't believe your mince pies when you see what's going down in the Nag's Head pub.

Yes, it's time for a knees up in the Nag's head. Meet the gang at the bar, choose a character & be the first to collect all the drinks. On the way, avoid being barred & take a chance on the quiz night or, if you're feeling lucky, try one of Del's Dares.....he who dares...wins!

Your amazing Nag's Head Game Collector's Tin contains:
x1 playing board, x1 dice, x1 bag, x6 character playing tokens, x6 character game cards, x20 happy hour cards, x36 drink tokens, x20 Del's dares cards

Roll the dice and move around the board. When you land on an Angelina Ballerina space, draw a dance card, and then stand up and perform the depicted ballet step or position. The first person to collect 4 dance cards wins, but first they must perform a ballet routine using all 4 steps or positions shown on their cards.
Also includes a "Memory" variant in the rules, using the dance cards.
This is a collection of variants of 4 popular card games with an Angelina Ballerina theme. Included are themed variants of: Memory, Crazy 8s, Go Fish, and Spoons.
Players spin to claim the objects that match their playing board.  The first to get all 7 is the winner.

Load different small colored plastic bunnies into the game board and turn it on.  The board rotates, and the bunnies randomly pop/hop/bounce up several feet into the air, while the players try to catch them in hand held baskets.  Different colored bunnies are worth different numbers of points, so players must choose to either compete for the rare high valued bunnies, or grab a lot of the lower valued bunnies instead.

A humorous bug themed variant of Bingo, where players roll two dice and cover any of the pictures on their cards that match the images shown on both dice.  Certain die rolls force pictures to be uncovered.  Get all four corners covered, or four-in-a-row in any direction, and you win.

A Bingo variant where squares are covered when the player hears or sees a matching reading "sight word" revealed.  Provides practice for beginning readers to recognize 46 common words like "the", "when", "to", "is", etc.

A collection of three games, where kids search for treasures, collect silver and gold, or match colorful fish on the playing board.  Intended to exercise and reinforce basic math counting  and pattern recognition skills.

Cammy is a Yahtzee variant that tries to evoke a "Southern feel" by stating in the rules that it was played on southern plantations and later by New Orleans gamblers. It also has a steamboat scene on the cover. It is different from Yahtzee mainly by the fact that 10 dice are used, and the low score wins. 
Players roll 10 dice on the first roll. On the second roll they choose which of the dice to re-roll. Same on the third roll. After 3 rolls the 10 dice are divvied up into two groups of 5 dice. Each player has a sheet of paper with 10 categories. Players choose a category to score with each of the 2 groups. Each category must be finished after the player's fifth turn.

Nine cargo ships (with diverse, hidden cargo values) try to escape from three pirate ships (black, green and red) in the Caribbean sea.

Ships are moved in turns (3 cargo ships, black pirate ship, 3 cargo ship, green pirate ship, 3 cargo ships, red pirate ship) drawing wind cards from a deck. Each card shows wind direction and strength, and ships are moved accordingly.

Game end as soon as all cargo ships are captured or successfully escapes from the Caribbean sea.

The one who captured/saved more cargo value is the winner.

Take Chinese Checkers, sprinkle with penalty features, "stay put" handicaps, and a blind color selector and you have Hunch!  Like its name, your success depends on how right your hunches are.  You stay still, go forward, or go backward depending on the colors underneath the marbles, which the players select themselves using the Hunch Dial.

Expansion for the game Fanhunter Battles.

It allows to expand the world of Fanhunter, adding new troops and big Guys.

In this game "Schnappi" (a little green crocodile that became famous due to a children's song that made it into the charts) wants to seek rest and go to sleep in the middle of the board.
It's the goal of the players to move Schnappi over obstacles and finally into the middle of the board.

boardgame: Knights of the Zodiac Collectible Card Game
description: A collectible card game based on the Knights of the Zodiac animated series. Players try to defeat their opponents knight cards in battle or "take control of" field cards to amass 20 victory points and win a "contest". The first player to win two contests wins the game.

boardgame: Codename: Kids Next Door Trading Card Game
description:
From WotC Press Release:

CODENAME: KIDS NEXT DOOR TCG players start with a deck of cards featuring the five agents on 5 different color cards. On their turn, players decide if they want to engage in a face-off competition using their top card, or instead place that card into the Kids Next Door's tree house headquarters. Cards placed in the tree house help boost the power of cards that are played in future face-offs.

When cards are played in a face-off, the player with the higher number (which may get boosted by a card banked in the tree house) wins the round. The first player to win 5 rounds wins the game, ensuring an entire game can be played in five minutes or less.

And what happens when each player plays a same-colored card? Both players race to be the first to slap a giant panic button placed on the playing mat to determine the round's winner.

The game consists of a board and two dice; one with numbers 1 - 6 and one with cartoons (car - boat - train - cat - bird - pig). The board has 36 squares with holes in the middle. Every square is a combination of the dice (train + 1, pig + 4, etc.).

Throw the dice and see what combination comes up. Players try to find the combination on the board and whoever is first pins his colored pawn in that square. Winner is the one who pinned all his pawns in.

Secret training of the "Wilden Fußballkerle".

But where are they? First you have to find two characters from the team and then they must score a penalty.

Sounds easy, buy  it's not,   with Markus the goalkeeper and the Damp-Hammer-Chip playing against you!

One with a good memory and one who is able to prove his dexterity with the penalties will win the secret training.

boardgame: The Lion and the White Witch
description: Your goal is to be the first player to reach the Castle at Cair Paravel.  Along the way, you’ll encounter the White Witch and her minions of nasty dwarfs.  But you’ll be cheered on by the boosts of the friendly beavers, as well as the benevolent presence of the Great Lion, Aslan.  Cooperative play is part of the action because once the White Witch captures a player, the rest must move backwards until someone intercedes with an Aslan card.  The pitfalls are balanced by the shortcuts, but the true of heart will surely lead the way.

Based on the 1980s kids game show of the same name, 2-3 players/teams play several "skill games" involving the components (such as a mini ramp, golf club, etc.) and answer questions for spaces on the board. The first player/team to reach the finish wins.

Dating from the original 1970's Mission Impossible TV series, the aim of this board game is to try and discover the identity of your 'target', and blow his cover, and eliminate him - before one of your opponents does the same to you. The sole surviving agent at the end of the game is the winner.

This is a children's dexterity game where players have a real-time shootout. The board is a long plastic tray flanked with colorful western town inserts. At each end of the tray is a plastic six-shooter (one for each player) that are attached so that they can move freely across the width of the board. The guns are loaded with ball bearings on the top. A slide comes out the bottom and ends flush with the playing surface where a plastic cowboy is attached. When you pull the trigger, a ball rolls down the slide, goes through the legs of the cowboy and shoots across the tray toward your opponent's side. Each plastic cowboy has a tab by its feet that acts as a target. Hit it with a "bullet" and the cowboy gets knock down "dead". The fist player to shoot the other player's cowboy wins. Each player has a rock and a cactus to use as cover. The trick is that you have to be out in the open (and taking careful aim) to shoot, but also dodging left and right to avoid your opponent's fire.

This game is similar to Crossfire.

The object is to get as many matches as possible. You ask other players for bugs that match yours. If they don't have a matching card, they get to shout "Don't Bug Me!". Bug facts are included. Fun and educational game for the kids! Includes 52 game cards.

Chilkat is a Canadian game about Native Americans and is played on a replica of the ceremonial blanket of the West Coast tribes.  Represented on the blanket are 10 symbols which North American Indians used as clan or family crests.  These crests were usually inherited or gained by conquest.  The more crests, the greater the prestige of the family or chief who displayed them; consequently these titles were very jealously protected.

Using strategy and luck, together with their chiefs and warriors, players will roll the dice for positions, move warriors for offense and defense, and challenge opponents in “fights to the finish” for possession of the symbols on the blanket.  The winner is the one whose chiefs control all 10 symbols.

This game has been developed for a dutch railway maintainer called Prorail by Identity Games International B.V., Rotterdam, the Netherlands.

You have to collect six cities with six different colours. You can try to collect them yourself, try to block your opponents, remove blocks that your opponents have put on you and be extremely lucky to get all different colours.

Box contents
- 1 manual
- 1 folded gameboard (folded 6 squares)
- 6 player cards
- 36 city cards
- 24 obstruction cards
- 36 city markers (in 6 colors)
- 12 obstruction markers
- 6 train pawns
- 1 custom dice (4 green + 2 red dots)

Purpose of the game:
Each player attempts to collect 1 city marker of every color. The first to succeed, wins the game.

Quick game overview:
On their turn, players throw the dice and, depending on the color of the dot, act out their options.
- Getting a green dot allows the player take two action. They can move their train either 1 or 2 steps, each step counting as 1 action. They can also turn the train around, which also costs 1 action. A green dot can therefor be used for: 2 steps OR 1 turn + 1 step (making the turn first). After moving their train, players must draw an action card and do what it says. After this, the turn passes to the next player.
- Getting a red dot stop the player from both moving and turning, they also cannot draw an action card. Instead, a player throwing a red dot may draw an obstruction card. They may either play it immediately, or keep it for later. However, any one player may only have up to 3 obstruction cards. Throwing a red dot and having 3 obstruction cards already means drawing a new card and performing one of the four cards.

Game setup:
All city markers are placed on the cities with their corresponding names. The decks of city cards and obstruction cards are each shuffled. All players draw one card from the city deck and take the corresponding marker from the board, thus having collected 1 of the 6 different colors, needing to collect only 5 more.

Second Edition:

The classic JMG game is now re-made in second edition.  Joe Magic Games creates a more in depth search for extra terrestrial life.  The 2013 version includes a map board and open trading board, draw bag and bidding clips, 36 action cards, 40 map cards, 40 rumor cards, 48 equipment tokens, 12 map tokens, 55 evidence tokens 18 contact hexes, 6 player cubes, 30 small and 15 large money tokens.  It is for 3 to 6 players, ages 12 and older and takes 45 to 60 minutes to play.  Players compete to find evidence and start rumors of alien visitors.  Some rumors are valid, others are traps set for other players. Players choose from 5 different actions each turn: To travel a short distance by boat or train, a long distance by air, buy equipment, research rumors, or publish findings.  The game features simultaneous play, bidding for contacts, and putting your money on rumors either to bluff or share in the evidence found.

From the box:

Join the search to make contact with aliens!  Only one player knows if a sighting is real or not.  Only you can overcome governments, nature, and even run the risk of being committed to a mental institution and losing it all.    Will you travel half way around the world and spend all the money you have to find a weather balloon - or real aliens!  It's fast, it's fun and it's easy to learn.  Let the hunt begin!

Ages 10 and up

A sort of "game of goose" (or "snake and ladders"). 
It has no board, but a deck of cards (square tiles), shuffled and put face down to form the track at the beginning of the game: the first player landing on each card reveals it and follows the given instructions (e.g. "move one space backward", "nothing happens", "reach the last player", etc.); these effects are valid for the rest of the game. 
Each player has a pawn (shaped as a car) starting at one end of the track. The goal of the game is to bring your pawn to the other end of the track. 
To play, you simply roll a six-sided die, move your pawn exactly that number of spaces forward and execute the instructions on the card you've reached. After that, it's the turn of the next player.
Thus there's no decision to take in the whole game: simply roll the die and hope.
The track made with cards acts as a "modular board" (it is different in every game).
The game was licensed by the Walt Disney Company and so the cards show some of the famous Disney characters.

This is a Japanese wargame on the famous battles of Oda Nobunaga, one of the most famous personalities in Japanese history.

Published by Bandai.

This package includes 3 tactical games and 1 campaign game.
Okehazama, Anegawa, and Nagashino are the tactical games.
The campaign game has 4 scenarios, such as "Road to Kyoto", "Surrounded Nobunaga", "Doomed battles against Takeda, Uesugi", and "The hero, Nobunaga".

Legend of the Galactic Heroes is a wargame set in a anime universe.
It is based on the most popular Japanese space opera novel "The Galactic Heroes Legend", "Ginga Eiyu Densetsu".

In the distant future, the Galactic World human is divided between two major powers: "Galactic Empire" and "Free Planets".
The Empire is under the rule of a dangerous emperor and corrupt aristocrats, while the people of the Free Planets have lost the ideals of their foundation and are under corrupt populism. Both camps have no ability to destroy the enemy or to settle the war which has dragged on for hundreds of years.

But the times have produced two geniuses of war into the same period. One is "Reinhard von Lohengramm", the incomparable artist of war.
The other is "Yang Wen-li", called "Miracle Yang", the magician of war. Their appearance will change the era that they live in.

In this game, players command Galactic Empire or Free Planets Alliance. This game contains three types of space combat ship units (BB, CL, CV) and various units which appeared in the original story. This game combines three types of starships, "Battleship", "Galaxy Cruiser", and "Galaxy Fighter".

The game uses a complete original system. Players organize Fleets under the command of unique admirals by combining characteristics of Starships. Each counter in the game is an Admiral and is rated by his fighting skills.

You use resource points to create or buy ships and put them under your admirals. The game system is very close to "Hitler's war".

When you fight a battle, each fleet is placed upon the combat display.  A die is rolled for initiative and the phasing side then moves their ships. The tactical map has several columns, each denoting a range bracket. Players must determine how they will do with their ships. They can move once and fire or move twice and not fire. However, ships have certain ranges that they can be used at. Battleships can be used at long ranges, cruisers at medium, while carriers can be used at short ranges.



This game came out in Old Games Journal #40.
family: Setting: Legend of the Galactic Heroes

boardgame: 武田騎馬軍団 (Takeda Kiba Gundan)
description:
Takeda Kiba Gundan (Takeda Cavalry Corps) is a Japanese tactical Sengoku era battle game.

This is a kind of experimental game not an exact historical one.
Players RANDOMLY picks up his initial force and setup them blindly.

A multiplayer game of competition to land on the moon.

From the company description:

"The year is 1959 and you have just entered the 'SPACE RACE.'

You are Mission Control in this fast-paced and exciting game of early space exploration. There are 19 different missions to choose from, and you must decide which to try and when in order to stay ahead of your opponenent(s).

But the strategy doesn't end with just exciting space missions. There are satellites, research and investments to consider also. Proper planning here can make the difference.

The game also includes a brief historical synopsis so you can compare your plan with actual U.S. - U.S.S.R. achievements, which makes for an excellent solitaire game.

SPACE RACE is a paper and pencil game of moderate complexity for any number of players. Playing time is approximately two hours. A 10- or 20-sided die is required for play."

The game consists of a 32-page "Micro Game" sized booklet and two player aid charts. Several charts are located in the center of the rules booklet.

Expanded by:

    Space Race Expansion Kit

The Highest ground is a game that imagines a world that has a very long period variable climate. Where an alien species has, approximately every 800 Earth years, collected a band of warriors from the most advanced warrior state on Earth at the time, and transported them to this planet. There they are to achieve dominance over the local population, grow a crop for the aliens and, in the ensuing climactic holocaust, be almost wiped out and, therefore, not become a threat to the aliens.

At the time depicted by the game the [aliens] have just bagged a new group of Earthlings and taken them to the planet. This group is from 1979 and is a bunch of mercenaries on the losing side of an African civil war. They have been grabbed from a soon to be terminally untenable position. They have been taken to a planet where the society that has grown up around the survivors of previous recruiting drives consists of several warring states peopled by Medieval feudal kingdoms and knights, Roman legions, pony riding barbarians and Egyptian pharaohs. The game finds the few mercenaries, having allied themselves with the European kingdoms, at war with the local Roman empire.

Generic land battle strategy board game.  Box measures 20" x 11 1/4".  Single-fold mounted color game board with 21 x 19 one-inch-square grid superimposed to regulate movement.  Terrain represented includes Mountain, Forest, Plains, Fords, and Rivers.  Multi-square Airfields also represented.

Miniature plastic pieces include Tanks, APCs and Airplanes (six Tanks, six APCs and three Airplanes per side; complete set of each in Yellow, Brown, Green, and Blue).  The APCs are eight-wheeled turreted vehicles strongly resembling a USMC LAV.  Tanks resemble a cross between the US Army M-109 SP gun and an Israeli Merkava tank.  Airplanes slightly resemble early speculative depictions of a Stealth fighter with twin tails and an unflattering forward-swept projection on the leading wing edge.
  
All of these units are moved according to the roll of two six-sided dice.  Die results are added together; the sum ("dice units") is allocated among friendly pieces for movement.

Terrain costs vary for Tank and APC units.  Pieces may be moved in any direction including diagonally, but may not stack together or pass through an occupied space.

All unit types have a "range" of two spaces.  Tanks may not attack Airplanes (unless all friendly Airplanes are destroyed).  Airplanes must be "airborne" in order to attack (they must "take off" down the "runway" of their "airstrip").

Combat is resolved by die roll, "attacker" and targeted "defender" each rolling one die.  The result of subtracting low roll from high roll is referenced to a "Battle Chart" yielding a result of "Safe," "Disabled" or "Destroyed."  APCs are destroyed on a difference of "1" or more.  Tanks are "disabled" by a difference of "1" or "2" and "destroyed" by a differential of "3" or more.  Airplanes are "destroyed" by a differential of "3" or more vs. another Airplane or an APC or a "4" or "5" if beaten by a tank. Airplanes are "disabled" by a "1" or "2" differential vs. another Airplane or an APC.

Destroyed units are removed.  Disabled tanks are turned on their side.  "Disabled" tanks may be "captured."  A friendly or enemy APC may "capture" a "disabled" tank by moving adjacent and surviving until the next friendly turn.  At the start of the next friendly turn, the tank is turned rightside-up and is considered friendly to the capturing player.

Disabled Airplanes "crash land."  The "disabled" Airplane travels three spaces in a straight line (in any direction the owning player chooses) and is turned upside-down.  During this "descent" the Airplane may "pass over" other units, but the space it "crashes" in must be vacant.  An APC (friendly or hostile) may "recover" the downed Airplane in the same manner as a Tank is recovered; however, when the Airplane is turned rightside-up, it is immediately returned to a friendly Airbase parking space.

"Disabled" pieces may be attacked and destroyed instead of being captured if a player so chooses.

A unit may only inflict damage on the first unit to attack it in a given turn.  If a piece survives an initial attack and is attacked again, it rolls in defense as usual, but will not inflict any damage on its attacker even if its die roll is higher.

Airbases may be captured by disabling or destroying all hostile pieces on the Airbase and then occupying at least one square of the Airbase with a friendly Tank or APC.

The object of the game is to be the last player with a surviving APC unit.

Rules include options for a short game with less equipment.  A small paper insert, dated 1991, provided a simpler version using the same movement rules but capturing by simply moving a piece onto an opposing piece space, moving and capturing until all "dice units" have been used.

The box art painting accurately portrays the pieces inside.  One of the modernistic tanks advances firing, evading a near miss while the moon lights APCs crossing a stream to the rear in Ardennes-type terrain as jets swoop above.  The title "Battle Command" appears in large red letters all the way across the top of this illustration.  In smaller white letters across the bottom: "A Fast-Playing Game of Strategy, Daring, and Chance!"

In the lower right corner is the logo of "Precision Toy," white letters superimposed over a line of evergreens with the title and evergreens "reflected" oppositely beneath.  The back cover features a large black-and-white photo of the board and components.  "Precision Toy" was located in Julian, CA 92036.  The game was made in USA.

Based on the Houndcats animated series, this is a typical early 70's spin the dial and move game.  The object of the game is to be the player that reaches the most "trouble spots" at the end of the game ("Sparkplug to the rescue!").  A deck of cards is shuffled and the first card is drawn and placed on the appropriate spot on the playing board.  Each player then spins the dial once, and EACH player must move their playing piece with the intention of reaching the spot with the trouble card (the spaces on the spinner have a number 0,1,2, or 3, related to each players color so during each spin, each player moves their playing piece the number of spaces indicated for the color of their playing piece).  Pieces can move forward or backwards and any number of players can occupy the same space.  Once a player lands on the card that represents the trouble spot, the card is taken by that player and a new card is drawn and the process repeats.

Published circa 1890's, this is Milton Bradley catalog #4334.  The game is identical to regular outdoor croquet but is packaged in a small box and the supplied pieces allow player to re-create the game on a smaller table top.  Interior of the box includes both the rules and a diagram for the arrangement of the posts and wickets to accommodate a rectangular playing surface.  Mallets are about 8" long, balls are 1-1/4" in diameter, and hoops are 2" in diameter (and are supplied with small wooden feet so they will stand erect).

Played on a board of 8 x 7 squares. The 2 players have 12 cylinders with the top closed (3 small of 4 value, 3 medium of 3 value, 3 big of 2 value, and the 3 biggest of 1 value). They can be put one on another like Russian dolls. At the beginning of the game, the 12 cylinders are on the line before their owner (6 under the 6 others). The object of the game is to bring 12 value points on the line before the opponent. When a player takes a turn, he can :

     take one of his cylinders and make it "eat" a foe cylinder next to it (8 directions) of a size immediately smaller
     free the cylinders under a top friendly cylinder by moving it in an adjacent square
     simply move to an adjacent square , a friendly cylinder or group of cylinders in which the top cylinder is his


Players must add memory to tactics in order to remember what cylinders are under the others.

A winner of the 1994 Concours International de Créateurs de Jeux de Société.

A Golf game. 
A 9 holes board, 6 playing pieces, 5 different dice (one for each club type), Bunker cards, Trouble cards, banknotes and a set of papers to mark score. 
A hole course is divided into a grid of squares. The player taking his turn chooses what club (and then what die) he wants to use. He moves his marker according to the result of the die throw in direction of the hole, and so on until the piece lands on the hole square. He can move it vertically on the fairway, and horizontally or vertically on the green. He notes the number of strokes he used and the next player takes his turn. When everyone has played the hole, everbody starts the next hole, and so on until 18 holes are played (the 9 hole twice).
If the piece lands in a bunker, a Bunker card is drawn and executed. If the piece lands in trees or rough, a Trouble card is drawn and executed. If the piece lands in water or out of bonds, the golfer plays again (the bad stroke is counted). 
Money is gained for different reasons (hole winner, par done...). The winner is the one who gains the more money. Alternative game makes the player who played the less strokes the winner.

SCHMEAR! is the build-a-bagel card game with round cards.

Turn over the cards and match up slices and toppings in a contest to build the biggest bagel sandwich.

But watch out:  If you add a life preserver, a CD-ROM or an old tractor tire, your sandwich is wrecked and it's the next person's turn.

At the end of the game, score 1 point for each complete bagel,  plus 3 bonus points if you've got the biggest bagel of them all.

This is a Japanese wargame on the Battle of Midway, 1942.

Published as one of the Series: SS (Sho-Kikaku) in 1990.

boardgame: From Golan to Sinai: The Arab-Israeli Wars 1956-1973 – A Clash of Armor Module
description:
Containing six scenarios spanning three wars, this Module includes all the supplemental rules and data needed to carry the CoA system through the Arab-Israeli Wars. Each scenario includes an 8 1/2" x 11" map, a brief historical commentary and tables with all pertinent data extracted for the players' convenience.

Although this module was designed to be used with CoA system, the scenarios can be easily adapted for use with other tactical armor systems.
language_dependency: 3: Moderate in-game text - needs crib sheet or paste ups
family: Players: Wargames with Rules Supporting Only Two Players

description:
Back of the box says:
Set out on your very own fantastic, fun-filled treasure hunt. Use Whit's inventions to search for the Diamonds of Virtue. Be the first player, or team of players, to recover one of the Diamonds of Virtue, then return it to start and you win!! It's that easy.

Players try to collect three different cards to complete a diamond jewel they collect from landing on special spots. Movement is determined by dice and by landing on time warps that allow moving to other parts of the board.

It's a fast-paced, room-to-room race that will test your knowledge of history, the Bible and, of course, Adventures in Odyssey. Draw a Charades card, and step onto the stage in the Little Theater to give the performance of a lifetime. Move to the Room of Consequence...but only if you’re prepared to reap what you sow. Pick a Draw card, grab a pencil and get creative. But make it fast--your next move will depend on it! Be the first to leave each room successfully and you win!

This is a game in the 18xx system, set in the Western United States, and dealing with the building of the first transcontinental railroad and its aftermath.  It has many special features:

1. Bonds to encourage the first transcontinental railroads to build track along historical routes.
2. Land grants to add extra income.
3. Connection bonuses.
4. Special city tiles that add extra length to trains that run through a token there.
5. Refurbished trains.
6. Palace cars (trains that double the value of one city run through with another train)
7. Mergers (some forced, some optional)

Overall, an interesting attempt, but the bonds seem quite out of balance, so that victory in the game seems determined by who owns the most Union Pacific and Central Pacific shares after the first stock round.

"The Original Intersection TM" is the humorously intended title of a game that owes almost all of its creative inspiration to Parcheesi, the Westernized version itself of Pachisi.  The designers are simply experimenting to see how little they have to change to come up with their own game that will make them a million dollars in royalties, (or at least a tidy income so they can design MORE games without otherwise having to work) and thus, avoiding copyright infringement lawsuits.  As such, it IS a useful and instructive game, as it is almost certain that they would have made more money had they simply reproduced the original public domain version and sold it.

The game differs from Parcheesi in that it has a jazzier layout, where the players move in a more complicated journey around a mazelike path of concentric circles.  An additional option is the ability to play as a "partner" with other players, moving your partner's  pieces once yours are all safely "home".

Though the board itself IS reasonably attractive, this is purely a "novelty" game, purchased for no other reason than that probably nobody else had ever seen it.  It is one step away from a total "home production", in that the parts are made nearly as cheaply as possible.  Indeed, though it looks totally unplayed, the box is so flimsily designed, that it is bent in several places.  The tokens are push pins with the pins removed to save production costs.

Hunny Pot Picnic is a simple game for very young children.  The game includes a hunny pot with a spring operated spinning ball shaped indicator to determine whether you get the hunny and can play the turn, or whether the bees appear and you have to pass the turn.  There are circle discs (called Tokens) with Pooh's friends (Tigger, Piglet, Eeyore, Rabbit, and Roo) on one side, and a Hunny Pot on the other side.  There is also one Pooh Token.

Hunny Pot Picnic has two difficulty levels of play:

Level One: Is a simple collection game to collect the most Tokens.  If the Hunny Pot doesn't show bees when it is activated, you collect a Token. The player with the most Hunny Tokens wins.

Level Two: Requires some memory skills.  You collect Tokens with the Hunny Pot indicator, but the object is to get each one of Pooh's friends.  If you flip over a Token of a Pooh friend you have already collected, you turn it back over, so that other players can remember where it was and collect it.

From the website:

The idea of the game is to imagine yourselves out on the golf course playing a relaxing game of golf - but - it is a board game and is meant to be light-hearted and entertaining, so there are a few hiccups along the way - and - you must bear in mind you will be very much at the mercy of the dice.
Played as near to real golf as possible with 2 dice, 1 black and 1 white. The black for the fairways bar the four short holes. The white for the four short holes and all eighteen greens.

The black and white dice cards are used when each dice rolls a 'c'

All the various games can be played e.g Stroke play, Foursomes, Fourball, Match play, Stableford etc. Up to 4 players at a time. Serious golfers can deduct their handicaps.

Joy for all the family and friends. Fun to try and beat 'Dad'

Ages 8 and up

With a bit of luck from the dice five beads can be threaded onto the string so that the caterpillar quickly reaches the apple and may eat as much as it wants.

A threading and dice game with wooden game board for 2 - 4 players.

From the inside cover:
"Rules for wargames with miniature figures from the days of the earliest Pharaohs of the new Kingdom through the end of the "Dark Ages" - 1500 B.C. to 700 A.D. and slightly later."

This is a 68 page docu-bind style book.  Early Gary Gygax miniature rules with illustrations by Greg Bell and Mimi Roach.  Edited by Timothy J. Kask.

description:
From the rule book: "Spell-a-Word is a crossword dice game The object of the game is to build words and run up the highest point total before time runs out. The Winner is the player with the highest score after 6 rounds of play."

The letter-dice are rolled, and arranged so all players can see the letters and their scores clearly. The egg timer is started, and each player tries to spell words using only the exposed letters. These words must be recorded on a crossword-style scoresheet, which contains several spaces with word-value multipliers. After time runs out, both vertical and horizontal scores are taken, a penalty is applied for each letter left unused, and scores are compared.

Contents:
13 letter dice
1 dice cup
rule book
"Spell-a-word" blank crossword score sheets
egg timer

The aim of the game is to build a spacelab from several components. The players compete to bring up the most modules. Therefore they need to buy launch pads, rockets  or the shuttle and the components itself. 
There are different types of rockets that vary in their ability to carry one or more modules and how much weight they can accept. Rockets can be used only once, whereas the shuttle is reusable.
The game ends when one of the players picks the last module, rocket or extra card. The player with the most money (earned by successful installed modules) wins.
The components are very well done for a self-published game, the first edition held 211 copies.

Three to Five players compete in making the most money bartering about used cars. 
Everyone sells and buys some and you *try* to make the most out of it. This is not easy since every player has several cards depicting upgrades (few) and defects (PLENTY) that are played face down on the cars that are currently traded. 
The "lucky" winner of a bid collects the depicted worth of the new car from the bank plus any present upgrades, pays the agreed price to the car dealer and then he needs to pay for all the defect cards played upon this card. More often then not he has less money then before the deal. So now he's eager to sell one of his cars to someone else, the only way of getting "save" money.

Imagine a quite clean, well arranged house... 
Imagine now from two to six children living in this house who have of another idea in their head to leave their toys everywhere... Imagine finally Papa, Mama and Grandmother who follow them and make them clean up their toys! Here, you obtain CAPHARNAÜM, a game in which each one moves a child of which the goal is to be 1st to get rid of all its toy tokens. But each player also moves the adults with an  aim of annoying his/her brothers and sister (and to obtain peace!). CAPHARNAÜM is a game about family in which the players more "aguerris" will quickly see the interest to be combined punctually for the greatest misfortune of their adversaries...

Between 1861 and 1865 the American Civil War was fought. A bloody war in which over 600.000 soldiers lost their lives.

In Bonnie Blue it's your decisions that will tell if the soldiers in your squad will die or live.

Bonnie Blue is a miniature game where 2-6 players control 4-10 soldiers each.

Expanded by:


     Bonnie Blue vid fronten
     Bonnie Blue och Indianerna
language_dependency: 2: Some necessary text - easily memorized or small crib sheet

description: A expansion for Bonnie Blue that adds rules for Indians in the 18th century.

15mm tabletop game owing a nod to computer games like Rainbow 6 and Counterstrike.  Comes in a box with 11 miniatures, one 128-page book (the rules are about 14 pages - the rest is lists and background), two dice and a tape measure.

The heart of the game is an action point system with straight d10 rolls used to resolve actions.  Combat is a lengthy process where you have to hit, wound and beat an armor save (yes, it's Warhammer 40k all over again) and then see what injury you delivered. Armor and injury saves are not modified by weapons, so whether you took a hit from a derringer or an anti-material rifle makes no difference to the target - a rather silly outcome, but there you have it.

There is a point buy system, a lengthy compendium of weapons & equipment (many of them with quite unrealistic stats) and a number of light vehicles (civilian models primarily - no tanks or APCs).

Alternative Armies' second attempt at a game in their Ion Age universe, this transports the action to the world of Golgotha, the "last battlefield" of the Great War.  It also modifies the background found in the Firefight game which ostensibly takes place in the same setting.  It uses the Ion Age miniatures produced by their company.

There are 3 factions - the Confederacy, the Khanate and the Trators (a mixture of mercenaries and scavengers).  Players can play in either 15mm or 28mm scale - the army lists differ, with the smaller scale featuring larger vehicles like tanks.  A points buy system is provided - players will control a squad or two in a typical game.

The basic game system is fairly straightforward, using a 10-sided die and centimeters.  All players move (using jumpacs for extra speed), then all units fire, with the latter normally being simultaneous.  Roll to hit - on a successful hit, you inflict damage, generally by chipping away at the opponent's armor and wound totals.  Heavy weapons will generally vaporize a target in one hit and have enormous ranges - consequently they dominate the game.  Finally, there is melee, which, as is typical for a science-fiction game, is generally more lethal than ranged combat.

The game itself could either be purchased as a PDF file or as a bound rulebook of about 94 pages (11 of them rules with another 17 pages of optional rules, most of which are geared towards providing a campaign system).

Based on the obscure Summer 1963 game show, players are divided into teams of two. When it is a team's turn to play, one player is designated the "instructor" and the other is designated the "artist". The instructor chooses a game card not seen by the artist. The instructor has 60 seconds to describe to the artist how to draw the object on the card in terms not related to the object. The artist has to guess what the object is to score. Players play a predetermined number of rounds, and the team with the highest score wins.

Picture This is the only home game based off a series that aired only as a Summer replacement. Oddly, no copyright date appears on the box.

Building on the successful foundations of Biplane Barmy, Blazing Jets is a game of modern aerial combat, with the emphasis on quick play (ten minutes or less) and tactics over luck.

This game is purchased in as a printable download in PDF format from RPGnow.com.

Rules included : 81 page book, USAF Jargon Guide, 286 Counters, Rules for Vietnam and Gulf War I, Maneuver Charts, Play maps
language_dependency: 3: Moderate in-game text - needs crib sheet or paste ups

description:
Play out classic sea battles of the age of fighting sail using the Ship of the Line rules. The pdf has everything you need to play (except dice) including rules, maps and counters. Ship of the Line is for two or more players and a typical game will take an hour or less to finish.

Ship of the Line includes full rules for: All manner of ships; Unusual vessels; Fortifications; Islands, reefs, shallows, harbors and other terrain types; Ammunition types; Crew quality; Prize money and rules for campaign play; random scenario generator; Scenarios; Extensive notes.

Downloadable from rpgnow.com

[translated from the publisher’s description]
A magical game of chance

Who will be the lucky guesser and the first to manage to place all their tiles? A cheerful murmur rings through the magic caves: Each year the apprentices meet to compare their clairvoyant abilities. But only the best can win the magic contest.

Each child has their own magic equipment, which they place on the four different magic objects and five tiles. Each round two children play a game with one another. One child secretly divides his magic objects between his two hands. The other chooses one of the hands and looks at what it holds: Are there magic objects whose tiles they hold? If so, they can put them aside.

This is the original version of Doctor Faust. It is themed as a Schnitzel hunt, or 'paper-chase' around an oval track. The animals chase after a wooden 'fox and cooking pot' piece. Each turn you have 7 points to use for placing a card, moving the fox and moving one of your own two pieces. Cards are placed face down next to the track and another player landing on one must end his turn, reveal the card and move the indicated number of spaces (1,3,5,7 or 9 spaces back, 2,4,6 or 8 spaces forward). 
If the fox reaches any player's piece or a player moves his piece up to the fox, a score card is taken. One is also taken each time the fox is moved over one of the two 'fox' spaces on the track. The game ends when the last score card is taken.

A collection of five human anatomy games, designed for children 6 on up. There are different levels of complexity for each game. You draw cards relating to the different body parts and attempt to place it into the correct position, and at a higher difficulty answer basic anatomy questions. At times you will draw a body breakdown card that will force you to discard the afflicted body part. This is an educational game designed to teach children basic and not so basic human anatomy. Even parents may learn something new.

From the game package:

Omashi is the fun new game of sticks and stones that can be played many ways almost anywhere. It's easy to learn and will provide many hours of enjoyment for your family and friends

Omashi is also a game that allows you to use your own imagination to create whole new ways to play it! So if you think about it, when you have the game Omashi, you don't have just one game - you have many games in one.

Omashi, the addictive low-tech game for the 21st Century and beyond.

In 5 Score, players try to create combinations of numbers and colors from cards.  There may be multiple ways of scoring; the players must try to set up their own scoring opportunities while depriving their opponents.

Game components: Game board map of Britain, 6 metal steam locomotive playing pieces, route cards, incident cards, dice and a dice shaker.

Object of the game: Travel from a south coast town to a northern town and back.  The route cards determine which towns must be passed through.  The incident cards contain events that can hinder movement.
Object of the game is to collect 6 cards which, put together, form a picture of a pig. then, to complete the course and undertake the final test at Chauvinist Pig's tail ...

A charades-based game where performers roll one or two dice to determine the physical challenge(s) - like “stand on one leg” “eyes closed”, "hum a song", "hand on head", etc.  Given the physical challenge(s) the performer attempts to act out a short list of words (in 30 seconds).  Get teammates to say the words and your team scores.  If not, your opponents can steal the card on their way to collect five cards.

Greek MYTHS & LEGENDS To Learn While You Play

A deck of 52 nicely illustrated cards consisting of 13 Greek myths, with 4 cards per myth. The object of the game is to collect the most points by collecting myth cards telling the stories. It is played in the manner of The Game of Authors or Go Fish.

For example, the Voyage of Odysseus myth includes cards for Odysseus, Scylla, Charybdis and Penelope. A phrase that is one-quarter of the myth is told on each card's text.

Other myths include Zeus Conquers, Heracles' Labors, Apollo's Island, Aphrodite Marries, Athena's City, Jason & the Argonauts, Persephone Kidnapped, Leda & the Swan, Helen Abducted, Theseus Kills Minotaur, Rhea's Trickery, and The Afterworld.

There is also a pronunciation card: Charybdis = keh RIB dis, Elysian = ee LEESH en, etc.

Ancient naval conflicts during the Age of Antiquity (Sparta, Athenians, Persians, Phoenicians, Romans).

This is a tactical naval battle game with flat models - Bimarines and Trimarines with arbalists, catapults, Greek Fire, and corvuses.

The game appears to come with only one scenario.

Instructions are provided on how to construct the paper ships (coloring and mounting).

The map area (not provided) would be set to a grid 36" x 36" with 3" squares in which to place terrain (not provided) and your ships.

Game duration is between 5 and 10 turns.  With a turn the following events take place;
1) Check Wind & Weather changes.
2) Determine which player conducts an Action (roll a die or flip a coin).
3) Ship or Fleet Action is conducted only after rolling dice and comparing the results to Ship Efficiency.
4) Combat occurs during an Action.  It is usually simultaneous.
5) Actions are continued until every ship has had an opportunity to take an Action.
6) Adjudicate loss of ships from fire, ship capturing, etc.

description:
Player 1: We've got a situation here, Colonel. Our embassy personnel in the Hyraxian capital city have been kidnapped by a rebel faction and are being held hostage. The local government can't do anything about it; the rebel influence on their ruling council is strong enough to veto any action. So you and your Rangers are going to take some action. You've got four C-130's and your ready-response battalion, and we can get you some flying air support if you think you need it. Load 'em up, move fast, rescue the hostages, and bring 'em home. If you can't fly them out, we'll set up a rendezvous in-country to get you out by choppers. Rangers, lead the way!

Player 2: Success! The American ambassador and his assistants will make excellent bargaining chips in our struggle. And the existing government can do nothing; our political operatives have seen to that. You, Major, must see to the safety of our "guests." You can expect a rescue operation from the Americans -- they have a long history of meddling in our nation's affairs. Colonel LeMeow of our Intelligence section will take command of the prisoners tomorrow. Until then, guard them well.

Rangers! is a 2-player Expansion for the free wargame Gators! depicting a modern US Army Ranger airborne operation.

This is the 5th of the "Bonsai Game" of daVinci, complete games on a single card (board on a side, rules on the other side).
The board is divided in 12 squares, containing snakes (negative points) or apples (positive points). The players alternates in placing two fingers of one hand on two free contiguous squares. For each covered apple, the player scores a point, for each snake, he lose a point.
The first player places on the board the fingers of the left hand, then the second player places the fingers of both hands, then the first places the remaining hand, and then the players alternates removing and re-placing any hand.
The winner is the first that scores 30.

Players compete moving the special "Sensotrons" around a map of Europe searching for the locations of hidden minerals, shipwrecks and treasure.

Once you have moved the number of moves shown on the throw of a dice you have the chance to answer a question. Answer correctly you move again and answer another question.

When your "Sensotron" locates some treasure if you answer the next question correctly you gain gold bars as a reward, if you answer incorrectly you gain a bent spoon (this is Uri Geller's game!).

The player that gains the most gold bars wins the game.

Forty-eight card deck rummy game in which each card has a factoid about the corresponding planet, sol, comet, or asteroids.  Seven card hands, play to 100 points.  Asteroids are wild.  Unusual mechanism is use of comets to "crash" into one of an opponents cards placed face down.  If a planet is hit, that card must be discarded.

From the box: "The dots on the dice determine how many rabbits jump, one after each other, from the back to the front. If there is an egg in one's supply, of the same color as that of the rabbit that ends up at the front once a jumping round has finished, the egg can be returned to the box. 
With a little luck with the dice and some tactical thinking you will soon be able to return all their eggs. 
For 2 - 4 players. 
Two ideas for dice games, with a little bit of tactical thinking."

From the author of Galloping Pigs, this is a fun "jump-over" game aimed at the younger audience!

From the introduction: Combat Commander 1973-1983 is designed to simulate modern warfare, using military miniatures. description:
Every agent chooses a secret nickname made up of equal number in words. In order to win players must guess the nickname of one of their opponents agents or kill everybody. In turn each agent must choose a movement (by foot, by car or by motorbike) an if they want to, an action (to catch, to shoot, to place a bomb, to be hidden, to try steal files). The actions allow you to get one letter of the nickname out of the victim and in some cases cause wounds.
The 16 parts of modular board allow you to make countless combinations for the city.
The game is set in an ideal city of 400 spaces.

If you've ever been intrigued by that game of futuristic land combat by a company whose name rhymes with "Hames Nerkslop," but were put off by the cost of figures or the time and skill needed to paint them, this game might be just the ticket. Instead of expensive figures, you use free downloadable counters. Instead of a large gaming table filled with hand-made scenery, you use my M8 maps. If you want a bigger army or a larger map, just download another page and print it. Time-consuming movement and combat rules have been replaced with fast-playing hex-based rules. And all the occult stuff in the original game has been replaced with... nothing at all.

If you're unfamiliar with that game, the premise is straightforward: buy soldiers, vehicles and weapons with the build-points given to you, then fight an enemy of a different alien race. You can also "buy" experience or special skills for your troops, or add a Hero if you think he will help. Some races excel at crunching close combat, while some prefer a fast-moving style; some fight in low-tech swarms, and others prefer high-tech gadgets. Vehicles range from one-man scout bikes to hulking super-tanks. The size of the battle can be a squad of foot soldiers, or a company of 150-plus men with dozens of vehicles, or anything in between. Several basic scenarios are provided with the rules. Almost everything is definable. 


StarMarines is a Free game based off of War Hammer 40,000

The Krylons invaded New Jersey one day. They had nothing personal against the Garden State, but they wanted to conquer New York City so they’d have a place to park, and they needed a base of operations. The Jerseyites were annoyed enough at being invaded. But being told that their state was just a stepping stone to New York was the last straw. As one, they rose up to fight for their homeland.

They didn’t have much to fight with. The federal government refused to intervene, because the Krylons’ green cards were all in order. The National Guard could help, if they could mobilize in time. Some of the state’s more militant citizens needed no encouragement to take up arms against the alien invaders. And a mad scientist in Newark (every cheesy sci-fi story needs a mad scientist) figured out how to turn pleasure boats and junk cars into impromptu spacecraft. But the aliens had big advantages in firepower and mobility.

On the plus side, the Krylons didn’t bring much of their advanced technology to bear. Their code of honor forced them to fight at the level of their intended victims, and they turned to the New Jersey media to determine what that level should be. Luckily, the Krylons’ cable TV had been cut off due to a billing error; they could only get one channel on their TV sets, and that channel was running a barbarian-movie festival that day. So the alien spaceships began disgorging their equivalents of Cossacks and Roman legions, thinking them adequate for the task of subduing the Garden State.

Boy, were they in for a shock.

Clockwork Stomp is the first supplement for Kenzer & Company's acclaimed actual-size miniatures wargame, Fairy Meat.

Gnomes: Clockwork Stomp contains background information and rules for gnomes, all-new gnome and fairy weapons, special gnomic equipment, status cards and counters, and battle scenarios.  Fully compatible with Fairy Meat, Clockwork Stomp is also self-standing game in itself, complete with all the gameplay rules you'll need to load up and march into battle against other wee folk!

Slow, weak, and plodding, gnomes make up for their physical shortcomings with deadly ordnance, sinister gizmos and swift trigger fingers.  With weapons ranging from the simple Iron Pistol to the horrifying Chainkiller Autocannon, these bearded brains are certainly nothing to laugh at!  And with the addition of a mechanical limb or two, even hand-to-hand problems can be overcome- with some small sacrifices...

The gnomes are joined in battle by their mechanical minions, the clockwork fairies.  With a mechanical, propeller-driven body and a living fairy head, the gun-toting clock-fairies provide speedy support for their gnomic masters' field operations.  Rules are also provided for Rogue Clocks, liberated Clockwork Fairies for use in your own Fairy warband!

Also included are rules for the magic-wrench-wielding gremlins (foes of anything with moving parts) and an array of gnomic equipment, including toxic Faecide Foggers, Pipe Bombs, and the short-lived Hatbots.

Expands:

    Fairy Meat

Sugar and Vice is the second supplement for Fairy Meat, Kenzer & Co.'s acclaimed 1:1 miniature game of cannibal fairy combat.  Sugar & Vice introduces two new breeds of Wee Folk: the decadent Moon-Fairies and the vulgar Pixies!

Lunar descendants of the old Fae royalty, the Moon-Fairies share a common ancestry with the Glitters of Earth.  Unlike their earthly cousins, they've progressed (or perhaps degenerated) into a glamorous Rock & Roll-loving fairy kingdom.  All goes well until they decide to check up on the Earth they left behind so long ago, only to find their former subjects have become murderous, barbaric, and frighteningly powerful.

To cope with the power Fairy Meat gives the insubordinate "natives", the meat-intolerant Moons have developed a magical lip gloss known as Pink.  Pink allows them to kiss dead fairies rather than eat them, vampirically soaking up the corpse's powerful fairy essences.  This addictive cosmetic gives them a much-needed edge on the battlefield, but it carries a terrible price; with the deadly risk of Overkiss, every kiss might be your last...

Sugar and Vice includes:
Rules and background for musical Moon-Fairy bands, Pixie mercenaries & gangs, and reality-warping Glamour Chunks!
More weapons, Twinkle spells, wands , and (yes) magic items!
Tasty new full-color counters!

Sugar & Vice provides more equipment, troops, spells and game options to all Fairy Meat players, expanding the game while keeping gameplay as swift and bloody as you've come to expect.

Expands:

Dogfaces in Space is an individual-soldier-level space combat game for 2 players. Choose the size of your battle, then fight it out. This game can be seen as an extension of my space/land battle game, Orbital Battle.

boardgame: Can You Make It as a Backpacker?
description:
Can you make it as a BACKPACKER?  That's the challenge of a new adventure-filled board game that tells you if you’ve got what it takes to travel the world.

Do the Visa Run, find your accommodation, and then hit the bar but keep an eye on your bag! Discover the must-do traveling experiences and the pitfalls to avoid, see the sights and survive the rickety public transport - all whilst racing across six continents with your mates. The first to travel the world in one piece wins the game

Just like the real thing, you have to travel on a tight budget - but don’t worry, there are plenty of opportunities to work and even phone home to bag some extra cash from Mum and Dad! You'll realize how quickly your money runs out, learn about the scams, the transport, the toilets... It's as close as you can get to the real thing without the food poisoning.

Designed by Marcus Lane, an experienced traveler, the game offers a backpacking insight that is difficult to grasp from books alone. Marcus says, “When I was knocking back a home-made rocket fuel in a remote Asian village one day I realized what a fascinating yet surreal world I was traveling in. I had read books that touched on some of the real issues of backpacking but nothing came close to telling me the full story. I decided to find another way to teach people what they were in for. It's as close to a trial run as there can be. You'll see how challenging yet fun the experience can be as you travel around the world meeting lots of new people and consuming all sorts of strange foods.”

Can you make it as a BACKPACKER? is quite simply the fun way to learn how to plan a safe trip.

Great for gap year students, career break gappers or anyone who fancies a trip down memory lane. Contains a gameboard, 6 backpackers, 224 Visa Run cards, 156 Continent cards, a Foreign Embassy, a wedge of doofers (cash), a dice and rules.

No cockroaches included!

This game is no longer in production.
If replacement pieces are needed, please contact SimplyFun. We'll do our best to replace game pieces as long as they are available in our warehouse.

In Dead Slug, players roll five six-sided dice and, if they can build certain shapes, continue to roll and accumulate points. It plays very much like many bar room dice games.

You need two+ players, five six-sided dice, and a way to keep score.

On your turn, roll all five dice. Pull aside only the highest dice rolled (including doubles), AND any ‘attached’ dice. An attached die is one that is sequenced one number lower than the high die (or dice). Ex: rolling two 6’s, a 5, and three 2’s, only the 6’s and the 5 are pulled aside. Rolling a 4 and four 1’s, only the 4 is pulled. Note that 1 has no lower attached dice (just as you can’t roll higher than a 6).

These ‘pulled’ dice occupy imaginary tiers in a ‘shape matrix’ (see completed shapes in the image section). Whatever your highest die is (or dice are), place it (or them) into the topmost tier of what will become your shape (if completed), and place any attached dice in the next tiers down. Ex: rolling a 6, two 5’s, a 2, and a 1, the 6 and the 5’s are pulled into the top two tiers (the roller is on her way to several possible shapes—Praying Mantis, Snowman, etc.).

Pick up any dice that aren’t pulled into the shape matrix and roll them again. The tier system continues, as long as the roller has thrown any ‘attached’ dice that would occupy the next lowest tier. Only the lowest filled tier is considered ‘open’ for attached dice, in that you can match the number occupying that lowest tier (in addition to rolling the next lowest number). Ex: the first roll ‘pulled’ three 5’s, which all occupy the top tier (possible shapes include Little T, Beret, Dead Slug, etc.) – the other dice (we’ll say a 2 and a 1) are picked back up and re-rolled. Second roll: another 5 and a 6 (the 6 cannot be placed above the 5 and will be re-rolled); the 5 is pulled alongside the existing 5’s (roller is on his way to a Dead Slug or a Beret). Third roll gets a 1. The roller’s turn ends and the dice are passed to the next player. Had the roller gotten another 5 or a 4, a shape would have been created and the roller would receive the appropriate points and would be ‘still rolling’.

Keep rolling, as long as at least one die can be pulled (from each subsequent roll) and played legally. Only if a shape is completed: players may decide to stop and score the points for the shape, OR roll all five dice again, continuing to accumulate points. If a player ever decides to keep rolling and is unable to complete a shape in subsequent rolls, that player ‘bombs out’ and scores zero points. A player must accumulate at least 2,000 consecutive points before ‘being on the board’ for the first time only. Once players are on the board, they may choose to score anytime they complete a shape.

To win: score 10,000 points. Longer game? Score to 20,000, etc.

Variation: For a slightly longer (but more exciting) game, have the Black Widow and the Skull take away 800 points apiece, BUT only if they are rolled completely on the first roll (they can still be ‘built’ for positive points). Have fun!

STOPS is an acronym for states, territories or provinces.
The STOPS AMERICAN EDITION has 51 STOPS (or 51 possible answers), the names of the 50 states and Washington, DC, all of which are printed directly on the colorful, dynamic game board. STOPS provides you with a clue, and you try to identify the STOP.

From an official report:

"The award-winning trivia game that is challenging without being frustrating. Featuring a variety of questions that vary in difficulty, STOPS© is a game that can be played by geography experts and novices at the same time. The questions range from obscure facts that might be more suited to Jeopardy, to easier fare about landmarks, capitals and more. Testers gave high marks to the design of the board, and the simple directions. Many of the teachers at the testing centers told us that they believed STOPS© would make a great tool in their classrooms."

Colledge is a drinking/party board game for coed players, aged 21 and over (due to adult themes and alcohol). The object of the game is to reach the top of the board and graduate from college first. When players land on certain squares, they must draw cards, collect money, or make rules that must be followed by the other players. EasyStar is a 2-to-4-player tactical starship combat game that allows you great flexibility in designing your spaceships, without having to print out or erase worksheets for your ships every time you play. It does this with a system of track charts and counters. All you need to provide is one or two six-sided dice. The emphasis is on fast play and blowing things up, not on long, drawn-out slug fests. I created this game because I wanted unlimited variety in ship design and tactics, without getting bogged down in range measurements, turning angles, power factors, or checking off gazillions of ship-damage boxes. Game features include:


     Reusable track sheets for ship design and keeping score
     Up to 18 ships for each player
     Five different sizes of starship, from very small to excessively big
     Speed and weapons capacity for each ship type can be altered
     All weapons can be custom-fitted for range and power
     Beams, missiles, torpedoes, and point-defense weapons

     Quick combat resolution - usually 1 die roll per weapon

     Ramming attacks and accidental collisions

description:
The Simple Large Outer-space Battle System lets you fight very big space battles. If 42 ships and 12 fighters isn't a big enough battle for you, then print more sheets of counters and double your fleet, or triple it, or quadruple it, even! There is no record-keeping at all; the emphasis is on fast play and interstellar mayhem.

S.L.O.B.S. III is a complete game, with maps and counters. All you provide is two 6-sided dice.

My All the Ships project is as close as it probably will ever get to its goal: to portray every major combat ship in the world, from the late 1890's to the end of WWII, as a free downloadable counter. So far, there are over 6000 ships represented, not counting aircraft. One thing that I didn't include is a set of rules, since I figured that most naval-game buffs already have a rule set in mind, and are using my counters to beat the cost of ship miniatures. But all the rules I've seen are too complex for my taste, which means I can't play a game with my own counters. So, just for the heck of it, here are some simple rules for naval engagements.

Easy Ships assumes you have some of my All the Ships counters, although a set of naval miniatures could work. You'll also need hex maps of open sea, like the "Open Sea" map tile that comes with this game (which is from from my M8 map system). These rules are meant for simplicity and fast play, not for extreme realism. The rules emphasize surface combat. The intended era is the first half of the 20th century. All you provide is some 6-sided dice, and pens and paper for some simple record-keeping if you use carriers or submarines.

Might of Arms is a set of rules for battles using miniature figures for armies ranging from the stone age through to the 15th century AD.  It focuses on large-scale battles rather than small unit actions.

MoA is a 108-page rulebook of which the last 40-odd pages consists of army lists.  It is aimed at 15mm gaming, but has options for 25mm and 6mm (1:300 scale).

Flashpoint! is a rule book aimed at 25mm squad level combat.  The 200 page rulebook contains not only the rules to play, but also weapons specifications, troop types, body armor, accessories, mechanized units and robots.  Flashpoint! is set on Earth in the far future (2247), its background is a cyberpunk environment with cartels fighting each other.  Players spend share points building up their units before going into battle.
Endorsed by Howard Jarvis, author of California's "Proposition 13" tax code change in the late 1970s. Object of the game is to "become a TAX LOOPHOLE EXPERT" and thus gain the most money.

Players compete at finding deductions while avoiding criminal and civil fraud charges - and jail time - during the audit cycle. Deductions can be challenged by the I.R.S., or the other players. As noted on the box, the object of the game is "to claim every possible deduction and end up as the taxpayer with the largest refund."

Players go around the board 12 times making investments, finding (and buying or selling) tax loopholes, shunting funds off to their Swiss Bank Accounts, and - of course - paying taxes. At the end of the game each player fills out a final Tax Form. The player with the greatest Net Worth is the winner.

Samurai wargame rules is a lightweight set of rules focusing on 25mm squad-level combat from 1100AD to 1500AD in Japan where a single figure represents a single man with its own unique fighting ability.  Samurai wargame rules simulates the skirmishes between the private armies of rival Daimyos.  It is an 18 page stapled rulebook.

description:

The rules present both a basic as well as an advanced game, with the advanced game adding homing missiles and crew experience into the mix.

Plunder is a vintage game with spiffy metal ships and metal coins, marred only by somewhat fuzzy rules.

Players take on the role of pirate ships and merchantmen in a race for the Treasure at the end of the board. Merchantmen pay tribute into the Treasure when they land on orange spaces; pirates pay when they land on green spaces. Players can take 5 Pieces of Eight as ransom out of the Treasure when they land on a Free Spot. They may also opt to pay any ship that passes them 3 Pieces of Eight or retreat their ship to the designated retreat point for that leg of the board.

When one player reaches the Treasure space by exact count, that player collects the Pieces of Eight and Doubloons amassed in the Treasure. One assumes that the game ends at this point and the player with the most value in coins is the winner, but the rules do not explicitly state this.

"England Expects" is a rare, "vintage" Card Game with a British Royal Navy theme published by Pepys around 1940.  It features beautifully illustrated cards depicting a variety of British military navy scenes, backed by a picture of Nelson's column.

description:
Horse racing game using statistics from real horses to simulate a horse racing season. Players attempt to purchase a stable of horses and enter them in races of varying distances. Each horse is rated from 6 furlongs to 12 furlongs with a number from 15 (no races at that distance) to 36 (world record. Building a stable is fun but betting right will win you the game.
Similar to a lot of the stat based sports games.

A game in which players make words from a central pool of revealed letters. By making an anagram of other players words, or adding letters, words may be stolen. Designed by the charismatic but maybe slightly loony Professor Julius Sumner Miller who hosted a children's popular science TV show in the 1970s in Australia. Consider this section from the rule book:

"It is obvious that longer words are still scarcer and harder to "operate on". But - armed as we are with this advice - we have put together A GAME where - to put it briefly - we make words out of letters and other words out of the words already made. And although on the face of it the scheme of things may appear trivial the fact is that this GAME can be made to possess and demand an uncommon intellectual gymnastic."

Premiering at the Origins Game Fair, Arclight is a fast playing spaceship combat game. This new release from Second Rat Games captures all the fury and chaos that dogfighting in space promises. With a one roll resolution system, an evolving setting, and the ability to add your own empire to the official universe, Arclight promises to be an excellent addition to any fighter jocks collection. Everyone wants to blow stuff up, and Arclight satisfies in style.

Fall of Constantinople was published in Panzerschreck Magazine #6 (Summer, 2001). The game consists of an 8.5 x 11 inch map, several dozen colorful but unmounted counters, and twelve pages of rules. The rules include many examples of play, several optional rules (among them two-player possibilities), and designer notes.

From the introduction:

A hypothetical scenario is included in which Toyotomi Hideyori's and Mori Terumoto's units participate in the main battle field.
In the historical scenario, the Western Army's response, units' defection and participation in the battle are determined in a specified turn. There is also a scenario in which all units of the Western Army participate in the battle.

Double Your Money is a board game from the 1950's, produced by Bell games and based on the very successful UK ITV game show of the same name hosted by Hughie Green.

The board has 4 distinct triangles. Each triangle reflects the player's colour - Green, Red, Blue or Yellow.  Each player place themselves at the start of their chosen colour. No two players can be in the same triangle. Movement is decided by the spinner in the centre of the board. A player moves along each row of their triangle. If a player's piece lands on the same colour square as his colour, he is asked a question. If he is right, he places his marker to the side of his triangle in the first prize money of 1 pound. Next time he gets a question right, he will double his money i.e. a progression of 1, 2, 4, 8, 16, 32, etc. (except when the prize money gets to 256 pounds, when it next jumps to 500 pounds so that the prize money could stop at 1,000 pounds.
The first player to gain the prize of 1,000 pounds is the winner.

The TV show, in its day, was extremely popular. Anyone winning the thousand pound prize was a celebrity (it didn't happen a lot). A thousand pounds, in the austerity of 1950's Britain, was a great deal of money.

Each player takes his turn to stick one of the rods in a hole not yet taken. When he hits a hole already taken, he must put back the rod and is disqualified. Winner is the player who succeeds to find an empty hole in the end.

To make the game more challenging each player may turn the board in any direction after having completed his move.

There are rules for movement, which depends on the unit, and the terrain moving through. Combat, which depends on the units, and the defenders position. Famous bridge personality Ely Culbertson's attempt to create an equally popular 2-person game. Jo-Jotte is named after Josephine Culbertson his first wife and distinguished partner. Unfortunately the game flopped and the couple divorced a year later. The game is basically Belote (i.e. 2 player Klabberjass, Bela) with the addition of Notrump, a Nullo bid, Slam bid and also bridge-like scoring above and below the line.

THE DEAL: Using a deck of 32 cards (A,K,Q,J,10,9,8,7 in each suit) six cards are dealt to each player. The next (13th) card is turned face up.

THE BIDDING: First, the non-dealer may accept the turned card as trump, or pass; if he passes, then the dealer may accept the turned up trump or pass. if both pass, the non-dealer may name any other trump suit (or notrump) or pass. If he passes again, the dealer has the same choice. A trump suit once named may be overcalled by a notrump contract, but not by another suit. Any contract may be doubled, and any double may be re-doubled.

THE DRAW: When the trump suit (or notrump) has been selected and doubled or passed, the dealer deals three more cards to each player and turns the bottom cared of the deck face up. The defender (player who did not select the final contract) must then (a) announce his intention to play a Nullo contract; or (b) declare his honor-meld, if any, against declarer's contract; or (c) pass. Declarer must then (a) bid a Slam or (b) pass. In either case, Dealer declares his honor-meld, if any. A slam bid will void any Nullo contact. Honor-melds are first declared by their point value; and the winning meld (see table) is shown and then scored. If played at the turned trump, that card may be taken in exchange for the seven of trumps.

THE PLAY: Defender leads first; he may lead any card. The other player must follow suit, if able; if she cannot follow suit, she must trump, if able. A trump led must be won, if possible. After the first trick, the winner of a trick leads to the next. In the trump suit, if any, the cards rank J,9,A,10,K,Q,8,7.  In any other suit the cards rank A,10,K,Q,J, 9,8,7. A Nullo contract is always played without a trump suit.

THE SCORING: Any winning meld is scored to the holder's credit in his honor column (above the line). Each player adds his trick score and honor score (including bonuses if any). Each player adds his trick score and honor score. If declarer has the higher total, he scores his trick score toward game (below the line) while the defender scores his trick score above the line. If the defender has the higher total, he adds declarer's trick score to his own, and scores the total below the line. On any doubled contract, the player with the higher total receives the combined trick scores, at twice their value, below the line. A game ends when a player scores 80 points or more below the line. A rubber ends when one player has won two games. When declarer and defender have the same total; or when a Nullo contract is played, the combined trick score is held aside and awarded above the line to the player who ahs the  higher total score on the succeeding hand.

On playing the King he announced "Jo". Later when playing the Queen she announces  "Jotte".  Without these announcement it does not score.  It does not matter if the card wins the tricks in which these cards are played.

HONOR MELDS
